title: New framework helps AI systems recover from mistakes and find optimal solutions
abstract: We introduce a new approach to agent programming, the development of LLM- based agents. Current approaches to agent programming often entangle two aspects of agent design: the core workflow logic and the inference-time strategy (e.g., tree search). We introduce probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN), a programming model that disentangles these two concerns, allowing the programmer to describe the agent workflow and independently experiment with different inference-time strategies by simply changing a few inputs. We provide an implementation of PAN in Python as the ENCOMPASS framework, which uses a Python decorator to compile agent workflow programs into a search space. We present three case studies that demonstrate how the framework lets the programmer quickly improve the reliability of an agent and easily switch between different inference-time strategies, all with little additional coding.
introduction:
Introduction

Recent work has shown the power of scaling inference-time compute for LLMs [1, 2], where popular strategies include best-of-N sampling [3, 4, 5], refinement [6, 7], and tree search [8, 9]. In LLM-based agents — systems that define how LLMs and other components interact to solve a task — these same strategies have become common ways of improving performance and reliability. Furthermore, several works have demonstrated the utility of applying sophisticated search and backtracking strategies in AI agents to improve performance in various tasks [8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14].

While various frameworks have been developed to simplify the low-level interaction between the program and the LLM [15, 16, 17, 18], a framework for agent inference-time strategies has been absent. Our goal is to develop an inference-time strategy framework: a framework that makes it easy to experiment with different inference-time strategies independently of the design and implementation of the underlying agent workflow. Such a framework is intended not to replace, but to be used in conjunction with LLM prompting and tool use frameworks, such as LangChain [15] or DSPy [16].

We target “program-in-control” style agents, where one defines the workflow in code and uses the LLM to accomplish specific subtasks [14, 19, 20, 21, 22].2 In these agents, inference-time strategies have traditionally been limited to sampling and refinement loops [6, 7, 19, 20], whereas more sophisticated strategies such as beam search and tree search have been rarely explored [14].

∗Work performed as a consultant for Asari AI 2This “program-in-control” style contrasts with the “LLM-in-control” style where the LLM decides the full

sequence of operations (tool calls) in the workflow [8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13].

Figure 1: An ENCOMPASS program specifies an agent workflow, which is compiled into a search space object, and inference-time scaling is accomplished through search over the nondeterministic execution paths of the agent workflow.

We identify the key bottleneck to be the entanglement of the inference-time scaling strategy with the core workflow logic when programming the agent. Programmers typically bake the inference- time strategy into the agent workflow [14, 19, 20, 22], which is inflexible, reduces readability, and limits the kinds of inference-time strategies that can be easily implemented. Therefore, we aim to design a framework that cleanly separates the representation of the core workflow logic from the inference-time scaling strategy. The programmer could then make minimal modifications to their agent to flexibly experiment with different inference-time strategies. Also, different agents would no longer require custom implementations of the same inference-time strategy, but can instead reuse a common implementation.

Our key insight is that inference-time strategies can be viewed as instances of search over different execution paths of a nondeterministic program. We developed the ENCOMPASS Python programming framework (“enhancing agents with compiled agent search”), depicted in Figure 1. Figure 1a and Figure 1b show an agent program and its corresponding workflow, respectively. The user specifies the “locations of unreliability” in their agent source code using branchpoint() statements. A location of unreliability is an operation such as an LLM call where repeated invocations produce outputs of varying quality. Since these different outputs give rise to multiple possible futures of the program’s execution, the program has a tree of possible execution paths. ENCOMPASS compiles the program into a search space object (Figure 1c) so that search can be conducted over this tree of execution paths to find the path with the highest score (Figure 1d). We call this programming model probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). As a form of angelic nondeterminism [23], PAN lets the programmer write their program pretending the unreliable operations always produce good outputs, and the runtime searches the space of possible execution paths for one where the operations indeed produced good outputs.

Our work makes the following concrete contributions:

We introduce the PAN programming model (Section 2.1), which uses angelic nondetermin- ism to separate inference-time algorithms (search policy) from the underlying logic of the agent (specification of the search space).

We present ENCOMPASS, a Python library that implements PAN (Section 2.2), providing 1. primitives like branchpoint() that the programmer can use inside their ENCOMPASS function, 2. a Python function decorator that compiles an ENCOMPASS function into a search space object at run-time, and 3. common search algorithms, as well as an interface for implementing custom search algorithms.

We illustrate how ENCOMPASS provides a unifying framework for common inference-time strategies and agentic patterns, which are special cases of search over nondeterministic execution paths of ENCOMPASS programs (Section 3). ENCOMPASS also provides a natural generalization of these inference-time strategies.

We present case studies showing how ENCOMPASS enables easy experimentation of various inference-time search strategies over an underlying agent workflow, allowing one to quickly identify the best-performing strategy (Section 4). ENCOMPASS opens up new possibilities for inference-time scaling of program-in-control style agents, where inference-time strategies that were previously considered too cumbersome to implement are now made possible by ENCOMPASS.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
EnCompass Advances Modular AI Agent Design by Decoupling Search from Workflow via Probabilistic Angelic Nondeterminism

News Article:  
“With EnCompass, we’ve separated the search strategy from the underlying workflow of an AI agent,” lead author Zhening Li stated in a recent MIT News report, highlighting a pronounced shift in AI agent design. This approach, articulated by Li and collaborators, introduces a mechanism for programmers to disentangle the workflow logic of an agent from the inference-time strategy—such as tree search or refinement loops—by leveraging a programming model they term probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). The result is a system where experimentation with search strategies is straightforward, code additions are minimized, and agentic behaviors can be explored and optimized with unprecedented modularity.

Historically, agent programming for large language model (LLM)-based systems has entangled the workflow—the procedural logic orchestrating LLM and tool use—with inference-time strategies responsible for output optimization. This latent coupling has limited both the flexibility of agent development and the reusability of strategic components; adapting or benchmarking different search approaches invariably required rewriting or restructuring significant portions of the core workflow logic. While frameworks exist to streamline LLM interaction at the prompt and tool level (e.g., LangChain, DSPy), a general-purpose, reusable structure for inference-time strategy selection and experimentation has been notably absent.

Probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN) is the technical mechanism underpinning EnCompass’s modularity. In essence, PAN enables a programmer to specify, within the core workflow logic, “locations of unreliability”—primarily LLM calls where output variance is a given—using explicit branchpoint annotations in the source code. Rather than encoding a hardwired sequence or a specific search protocol, the agent’s logic is compiled into a search space of possible execution paths, each path representing a particular combination of outputs at branchpoints.

The “angelic” aspect refers to the ability to treat these nondeterministic operations as if they always succeed from the programmer’s perspective, while, at run time, EnCompass conducts a search across possible outcomes, seeking execution paths that yield the highest performance according to a scoring function. Concretely, this reifies the agent workflow as a program tree; branches represent alternative LLM outputs, and the search strategy operates orthogonally to the underlying logic, traversing or pruning paths in accordance with policies such as best-of-N-sampling, beam search, or custom-defined heuristics.

This explicit separation between agent logic and inference-time strategy yields three core advantages, each documented in the source [Abstract, Introduction]:

1. **Rapid Experimentation with Search Policies:** PAN allows programmers to explore, compare, and interchange search strategies—including those previously considered cumbersome to implement—by simply modifying input parameters or decorators, rather than overhauling agent workflows.
2. **Minimal Code Additions:** According to empirical findings reported in MIT News and the technical paper, EnCompass drastically reduces the programming overhead required to integrate search, contrasting sharply with the boilerplate or ad hoc code often necessary when search logic is woven directly into the agent workflow.
3. **Reusable Strategy Implementations:** Because search algorithms are now modular and API-driven, a single strategy implementation can be reused across disparate agents, enhancing maintainability and fostering comparative research without duplicative engineering effort.

To illustrate the effect, the EnCompass framework leverages a Python decorator system. When applied to a program-in-control style agent function—a paradigm where the workflow is prescriptively authored in code—the decorator compiles this workflow, with its annotated branchpoints, into a search space object at runtime. Developers then select an appropriate search or optimization routine, either from a suite of pre-built strategies or custom algorithms via provided interfaces. Rather than directly modifying agent logic, the inference-time strategy is invoked as an overlay, facilitating quick iteration and empirical evaluation.

Case studies within the technical documentation showcase how this approach not only enables rapid strategy comparison, but also reliably improves agent robustness by systematically searching for execution paths where unreliable LLM outputs result in successful completions. Such flexibility paves the way for broader adoption of advanced agentic search and backtracking methods that, until now, have remained largely theoretical outside research prototypes.

By grounding agent programming in probabilistic angelic nondeterminism and operationalizing it through the EnCompass framework, the work of Li and colleagues marks a concrete step towards the modularization and democratization of robust inference-time strategies for LLM-based systems. For expert practitioners and researchers seeking to leverage, evaluate, or extend agentic reasoning at scale, the full technical details are accessible in [“EnCompass: Enhancing Agent Programming with Search Over Program Execution Paths”](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.03571).

“With EnCompass, we’ve separated the search strategy from the underlying workflow of an AI agent,” says lead author Zhening Li ’25, MEng ’25, who is an MIT electrical engineering and computer science (EECS) PhD student, CSAIL researcher, and research consultant at Asari AI. “Our framework lets programmers easily experiment with different search strategies to find the one that makes the AI agent perform the best.”

[Paper: “EnCompass: Enhancing Agent Programming with Search Over Program Execution Paths”](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2512.03571)

So just how code-efficient is EnCompass for adding search to agent programs? According to researchers’ findings, the framework drastically cut down how much programmers needed to add to their agent programs to add search, helping them experiment with different strategies to find the one that performs the best.

title: Discrete spatial diffusion models data while obeying scientific principles
abstract: Generative diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in producing high- quality images. However, these models typically operate in continuous intensity spaces, diffusing independently across pixels and color channels. As a result, they are fundamentally ill-suited for applications involving inherently discrete quantities—such as particle counts or material units—that are constrained by strict conservation laws like mass conservation, limiting their applicability in scientific workflows. To address this limitation, we propose Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a framework based on a continuous-time, discrete-state jump stochastic process that operates directly in discrete spatial domains while strictly preserving particle counts in both forward and reverse diffusion processes. By using spatial diffusion to achieve particle conservation, we introduce stochasticity naturally through a dis- crete formulation. We demonstrate the expressive flexibility of DSD by performing image synthesis, class conditioning, and image inpainting across standard image benchmarks, while exactly conditioning total image intensity. We validate DSD on two challenging scientific applications: porous rock microstructures and lithium- ion battery electrodes, demonstrating its ability to generate structurally realistic samples under strict mass conservation constraints, with quantitative evaluation using state-of-the-art metrics for transport and electrochemical performance.
introduction:
Introduction

Diffusion-based generative models have emerged as powerful tools for high-quality image generation [36, 66, 69]. Typically, these models inject noise into the images, then learn to reverse this noise- adding process to recover meaningful structure. In most frameworks, this is based on an Itô Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) with Gaussian noise. While effective for many vision tasks, these approaches inherently assume continuous pixel intensities, which can cause difficulty when dealing with the discrete nature of many datasets. Tasks beyond vision, such as those in the physical sciences have many applications which require discrete physical quantities, such as particle counts in a simulation, or phases in materials microstructure. Conservation of total quantities is critical for scientific applications, and so generative modeling which can operate under constrained, discrete pixel intensities would enable scientifically grounded and physically consistent synthesis. Such a capability might also prove useful within vision tasks, such as inpainting and super-resolution.

Scientific and engineering studies of the natural world using computational techniques often involve discrete variables in space and/or time. On microscopic scales, everyday materials exhibit extremely complex structural patterns which encode the history of their formation, and play a large role in how the material functions on a macroscopic level. An important and wide-reaching field of study is materials microstructure, which is used in materials design [35], forensic analysis, hydrology [7], energy storage [65], and even medicine, such as in studies of bone structure [53]. For example,

∗Los Alamos National Laboratory

crystal grain shapes can give rise to complex stress patterns which affect the yield strength of a metal [10]. Materials microstructures are frequently represented by a small number of discrete phases that describe their underlying chemical structures. In sandstone, for example, the overall arrangement of nanocrystals is highly disordered, and gives rise to complex pore structures, through which subsurface water flows. This microstructure has an enormous influence on the rate of transport of fluids and contaminants [6]. Microstructure of electrodes is also known to have an immense impact on the characteristics of electrochemical devices [58]. Small changes in thermodynamic properties can cause drastic changes in microstructure, such as in stainless steels [79], requiring the study of microstructure as a function of phase contents. Furthermore, gathering real-world data is often complex and expensive; decades of work have been applied to computational modeling of the generation and consequences of microstructure prior to the widespread popularization of machine learning [72]. While diffusion models have been constructed for microstructure [4, 25, 37, 46, 47, 52, 80], they have not handled exact porosity (intensity) constraints.

In this work, we introduce Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a discrete-state Markov chain-based diffusion framework in which the forward process redistributes discrete units of intensity in space (Fig. 1c). Unlike previous diffusion models, DSD exactly preserves total intensity throughout both the forward and reverse phases, ensuring that global properties—such as mass fractions—are exactly conserved by representing them in terms of conserved particles. We demonstrate that DSD enables scientific applications and can extend the capabilities of conventional image processing tasks, like image generation and inpainting in discrete domains. By directly modeling discrete transitions, DSD enables generative modeling under conservation laws, allowing models that specialize for constrained conditions in scientific applications and beyond.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Discrete Spatial Diffusion: Enforcing Conservation Laws in Generative Modeling for Scientific Domains

News Article:  
Conservation laws—such as mass, energy, and particle number preservation—are central pillars in scientific and engineering disciplines. These laws underpin the fidelity of mathematical models involved in simulating and interpreting fundamental phenomena across heat transfer, wave propagation, and fluid flow dynamics. Modern computational models tasked with representing intricate materials or multiphase systems must strictly enforce such conservation principles to remain physically consistent. In this regard, recent advances exemplified by “Learning Physical Models that Can Respect Conservation Laws” [3] demonstrate the tangible inadequacies of unconstrained neural approaches like the Attentive Neural Process (ANP): empirical findings, visually summarized in Figure 2, show how unconstrained ANP solutions permit unphysical mass flux, with system mass entering or exiting the domain over time.

This departure from conservation—a recurrent pitfall in neural surrogates and machine-learning-based scientific modeling—has significant implications. As detailed in the referenced study, the ANP produces solution profiles in which the conserved quantity (e.g., total mass) deviates from physical reality, lacking the guarantee that the modeled system behaves as a closed, physically consistent environment. This is apparent in applications where no boundary flux is present: the true mass, defined as \( U(t) = R \), should remain constant, yet unconstrained methods allow violations that undermine scientific trust and downstream utility.

The newly proposed Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD) framework responds directly to these limitations. DSD is grounded in a continuous-time, discrete-state jump stochastic process, purpose-built to operate on discrete spatial domains where strict preservation of physically conserved quantities—such as particle number or mass fraction—is non-negotiable. In contrast to standard diffusion models, which apply an Itô Stochastic Differential Equation with continuous intensities and independent channel-wise noise injections, DSD models forward and reverse diffusion as an explicit redistribution of discrete intensity units in space. This approach guarantees that total intensity (or mass) is exactly conserved across all generative steps, adhering to the foundational requirements of physical realism long established in scientific computing and classical finite-volume methods [Abstract, Introduction, Anchor Event].

The rigorous conservation achieved by DSD is realized by encoding stochasticity through discrete transitions rather than continuous perturbation. This enables the model to naturally capture uncertainty without introducing violations of global conservation. Empirically, DSD demonstrates expressive flexibility across standard vision benchmarks, supporting tasks such as image synthesis, class conditioning, and image inpainting, all under strict total intensity conditioning. More significantly, DSD is validated on scientific data representing porous rock microstructures and lithium-ion battery electrodes—domains where microstructure and phase content dictate macroscopic characteristics and where mass conservation is not only scientifically relevant but essential. The generated samples are structurally realistic while maintaining exact mass or particle conservation, as quantified by state-of-the-art evaluation metrics for transport and electrochemical performance [Abstract].

The conceptual and technical divergence from prior generative diffusion models is substantial. Where previous methods could only approximate conservation by post-hoc correction or soft constraints, DSD enforces it by construction. This distinction is non-trivial in application areas such as computational materials science, hydrology, or energy storage, where microstructural properties (e.g., porosity, phase fractions) are both discrete and conserved by physical law. The discrete redistribution mechanism of DSD ensures that, unlike conventional models—including those previously applied to microstructure generation [4, 25, 37, 46, 47, 52, 80]—the generated outputs satisfy conservation at every stage of the generative process, enabling scientifically grounded synthetic data production [Introduction].

DSD’s framework opens new possibilities for computational workflows in research areas conditioned on physical constraints. It provides not only a generative architecture better suited for modeling discrete and conserved systems, but also closes a critical gap highlighted by recent research on embedding physical consistency in neural surrogates. The result is a technical advance with broad potential for domains where strict adherence to conservation laws is a gating requirement for scientific validity.

For researchers and practitioners operating at the interface of machine learning and the physical sciences, the work behind Discrete Spatial Diffusion represents a significant step forward in constructing models that respect, rather than approximate, the invariants intrinsic to their scientific contexts. To rigorously evaluate the model’s architecture, empirical findings, and domain applications, readers are encouraged to access the full research article at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.01917.

While the introduction mentions conservation laws and ties them to DSD, it does not explicitly emphasize their critical role in scientific and engineering disciplines as strongly as required. Additionally, the narrative transitions from ANP to DSD are not as smooth or explicit as needed, particularly in explicitly referencing Figure 2 and its implications. The technical fidelity is robust, but the narrative flow and emphasis need refinement.

It mentions conservation laws and their importance but delays the explicit connection to DSD until later. Additionally, while the BRIDGE CONSTRAINT is adequately addressed with respect to ANP's violations and DSD's solution, the narrative could be smoother in transitioning from the problem (ANP) to the solution (DSD).

title: Rosetta stone for database inputs reveals serious security issue
abstract:
We introduce the first method for translating text embeddings from one vector space to another without any paired data, encoders, or predefined sets of matches. Our unsupervised approach translates any embedding to and from a universal latent representation (i.e., a universal semantic structure conjectured by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis). Our translations achieve high cosine similarity across model pairs with different architectures, parameter counts, and training datasets. The ability to translate unknown embeddings into a different space while preserving their geometry has serious implications for security. An adversary with access to a database of only embedding vectors can extract sensitive information about underlying documents, sufficient for classification and attribute inference.

Figure 1: Left: input embeddings from different model families (T5-based GTR [46] and BERT-based GTE [32]) are fundamentally incomparable. Right: given unpaired embedding samples from different models on different texts, our model learns a latent representation where they are closely aligned.
introduction:
Introduction

Text embeddings are the backbone of modern NLP, powering tasks like retrieval, RAG, classification, and clustering. There are many embedding models trained on different datasets, data shufflings, and initializations. An embedding of a text encodes its semantics: a good model maps texts with similar semantics to vectors close to each other in the embedding space. Since semantics is a property of text, different embeddings of the same text should encode the same semantics. In practice, however, different models encode texts into completely different and incompatible vector spaces.

The Platonic Representation Hypothesis [18] conjectures that all image models of sufficient size converge to the same latent representation. We propose a stronger, constructive version of this hypothesis for text models: the universal latent structure of text representations can be learned and, furthermore, harnessed to translate representations from one space to another without any paired data or encoders.

A1

Subject: Enron Bashing on FrontlineBody:Karen, please call me when you receive this email. Thank you. Rick

Embeddings of W(Encoder B)

Figure 2: Given only a vector database from an unknown model, vec2vec translates the database into the space of a known model using latent structure alone. Converted embeddings reveal sensitive information about the original documents, such as the topic of an email (pictured, real example).

In this work, we show that the Strong Platonic Representation Hypothesis holds in practice. Given unpaired examples of embeddings from two models with different architectures and training data, our method learns a latent representation in which the embeddings are almost identical (Figure 1).

We draw inspiration from research on aligning word embeddings across languages [61, 10, 15, 9] and unsupervised image translation [35, 68]. Our vec2vec method uses adversarial losses and cycle consistency to learn to encode embeddings into a shared latent space and decode with minimal loss. This makes unsupervised translation possible. We use a basic adversarial approach with vector space preservation [45] to learn a mapping from an unknown embedding distribution to a known one.

vec2vec is the first method to successfully translate embeddings from the space of one model to another without paired data.1 vec2vec translations achieve cosine similarity as high as 0.92 to the ground-truth vectors in their target embedding spaces and perfect matching on over 8000 shuffled embeddings (without access to the set of possible matches in advance).

To show that our translations preserve not only the relative geometry of embeddings but also the semantics of underlying inputs, we extract information from them using zero-shot attribute inference and inversion, without any knowledge of the model that produced the original embeddings.2
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Emergent Risks in AI: Unsupervised Embedding Translation Exposes Sensitive Data in Vector Databases

News Article:

The accelerating adoption of vector databases and text embeddings as the foundation of modern AI—encompassing retrieval, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and classification systems—has introduced a novel class of security vulnerabilities. A primary concern, now substantiated by recent research [https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.12540], is the exposure of sensitive information from embedding-only databases: adversaries no longer need raw textual data to reconstruct or classify private content. This threat is underscored in contemporary data governance discourse, which directly correlates the operational backbone of generative AI—vector databases and embeddings—with elevated data security risk profiles. The introduction of unsupervised translation of embeddings exacerbates these challenges, requiring organizations to reconsider and reinforce their security postures.

At the technical core of these emergent vulnerabilities is a methodology—vec2vec—that enables the translation of text embeddings between arbitrary NLP models in a fully unsupervised manner, without paired data, access to encoders, or predefined vector correspondences. The research leverages and substantively strengthens the Strong Platonic Representation Hypothesis: for text, a universal latent structure can be constructed such that embeddings from any sufficiently capable model can be translated into one another’s semantic space using adversarial losses and cycle consistency. The practical implications are significant: previously incompatible vector spaces, such as those produced by T5-based and BERT-based models, can now be systematically aligned even without shared vocabulary or training regimes.

The central technical breakthrough lies in vec2vec’s unsupervised translation pipeline, which achieves near-ground-truth cosine similarities (up to 0.92) between converted embeddings and their equivalents in the target space, and perfect matching in controlled shuffling experiments involving thousands of vectors. This process involves mapping embedding distributions into a shared latent space and decoding them to target spaces, preserving the relative geometry of the embeddings as well as the semantics of the underlying text inputs. Critically, the capacity to translate a set of unknown embeddings into a reference space—without knowledge of the original model—enables zero-shot attribute inference and inversion attacks, as documented in real-world scenarios like the reconstruction and classification of confidential emails using only their vector representations.

This technical advance spotlights the inadequacy of classical data protection paradigms when applied to AI-native infrastructure. Security measures—such as data encryption, which aims to render stored and transmitted embeddings unintelligible to unauthorized parties; access control, limiting visibility and mutability of embedding repositories; and anonymization or pseudonymization, intended to remove directly identifying features from stored vectors—are all rendered necessary, but not independently sufficient, in the face of unsupervised embedding translation. Since vec2vec recovers semantic structure solely from vectorial distributions, attackers can circumvent measures that do not consider the broader geometry and translatability of embeddings.

Likewise, regular auditing and monitoring are pivotal; breach detection must extend beyond traditional access logs to include anomaly and inversion attempts on embedding spaces. Embedding validation—ensuring that stored vectors do not encode sensitive or reconstructible semantic information—now demands embedding-space differential privacy, as adversaries are demonstrably capable of semantic inference with no model or text access. Secure model deployment protocols, including runtime isolation and routine model updating, further restrict the attack surface by limiting exposure windows of vulnerable vector spaces. Incident response mechanisms must now anticipate privacy events at the embedding level, not solely at the document or application layer.

Notably, these classical controls must converge upon adaptive, contextual access control mechanisms tailored for AI-native artifacts. The research makes clear that static permissioning is insufficient: permissions must be dynamically contextualized to ongoing usage, model evolution, and the continuously shifting threat landscape. Organizations managing generative AI deployments are thus impelled to implement semantically aware, fine-grained access regimes—bolstered by continuous validation—that address not only who can access embedding repositories, but under what contexts and using which workflows. This focus on adaptive, context-driven policy is recognized as an industry imperative and is central to the governance paradigm recommended in contemporary AI security frameworks.

Privacera AI Governance (PAIG) directly addresses these multifaceted risks by integrating a comprehensive security, safety, and governance apparatus for GenAI applications, spanning adaptive access control, embedding validation, and robust incident response. PAIG’s architecture operates independently from specific model, library, or retrieval strategies, aligning with the need for holistic, future-proof protection as evidenced by the demonstrated translatability risks of embeddings. By centralizing observability and enforcing policy at the embedding and vector database layers, PAIG enables organizations to operationalize best practices—dynamic data encryption, contextualized access management, continuous anomaly detection, and immediate incident remediation—thereby mitigating the practical risks detailed in the current state of embedding translation research.

The convergence of emerging embedding translation techniques and unresolved security risks compels organizations and researchers to rethink AI-native data protections. To fully comprehend the technical impact and operational ramifications, access the original research at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.12540.

anchor_event.summary: Search result [2] (Guide for Implementing Data Security Governance Programs) discusses securing vector databases and embeddings, explicitly referencing the backbone of generative AI and the associated best practices and risks. This aligns closely with the primary research's revelation that embeddings alone can be exploited to extract sensitive information, highlighting emergent vulnerabilities in AI-powered systems.
anchor_event.full_content:
1.   **Data Encryption:** Encrypt data both in transit and at rest to prevent unauthorized access during storage or transfer.
2.   **Access Control:** Implement strict access control measures to limit and monitor who can view, modify, or manage the database.
3.   **Anonymization and Pseudonymization:** Apply techniques to anonymize or pseudonymize sensitive data before storing embeddings.
4.   **Regular Auditing and Monitoring:** Continuously monitor access logs and perform audits to detect and resolve suspicious activities.
5.   **Embedding Validation:** Ensure embeddings do not contain encoded sensitive information using validation techniques.
6.   **Secure Model Deployment:** Deploy models in secure environments and update them regularly to address vulnerabilities.
7.   **Incident Response Plan:** Prepare and test a detailed incident response plan to address potential security breaches promptly.

Notably, open-source frameworks like LangChain have been designed to simplify the development of applications using vector databases and embeddings, offering developers powerful tools to streamline integration and functionality. While these steps enable the creation of powerful AI-driven applications, they also introduce security risks that must be addressed.

Organizations building GenAI solutions must prioritize adaptive, contextual access control mechanisms that go beyond static permissions and embrace the dynamic, evolving nature of AI models. In doing so, we can unlock the full potential of Generative AI while safeguarding the data and users it impacts. Privacera AI Governance (PAIG) is the industry’s first comprehensive security, safety and governance solution for GenAI applications. PAIG operates independently from your choices of models, libraries and RAG approaches to truly provide a holistic security and observability platform. You can start your journey with PAIG today by[scheduling a demo here](https://privacera.com/request-a-paig-demo/) or you can get started on the [open source version of PAIG](https://paig.ai/).

title: From brain scans to alloys Teaching AI to make sense of complex research data
abstract: Traditional entropy-based methods—such as cross-entropy loss in classification problems—have long been essential tools for representing the information uncertainty and physical disorder in data and for developing artificial intelligence algorithms. However, the rapid growth of data across various domains has introduced new challenges, particularly the integration of heterogeneous datasets with intrinsic disparities. To address this, we introduce a zentropy-enhanced neural network (ZENN), extending zentropy theory into the data science domain via intrinsic entropy, enabling more effective learning from heterogeneous data sources. ZENN simultaneously learns both energy and intrinsic entropy components, capturing the underlying structure of multisource data. To support this, we redesign the neural network architecture to better reflect the intrinsic properties and variability inherent in diverse datasets. We demonstrate the effectiveness of ZENN on classification tasks and energy landscape reconstructions, showing its superior generalization capabilities and robustness-particularly in predicting high-order derivatives. In image and text classification tasks, ZENN demonstrates superior generalization by introducing a learnable temperature variable that models latent multisource heterogeneity, allowing it to surpass state-of-the-art models on CIFAR-10/100, BBC News, and AG News. As a practical application in materials science, we employ ZENN to reconstruct the Helmholtz energy landscape of Fe3Pt using data generated from density functional theory and capture key material behaviors, including negative thermal expansion and the critical point in the temperature–pressure space. Overall, this work presents a zentropy-grounded framework for data-driven machine learning, positioning ZENN as a versatile and robust approach for scientific problems involving complex, heterogeneous datasets.
introduction: The rise of complex, heterogeneous datasets is transforming data science, enabling new capabilities across fields such as biomedical modeling, materials discovery, climate prediction, and the development of digital twins. These digital replicas of physical systems rely on the seamless integration of real-time and historical data from multiple sources, including sensors, machines, experiments, and simulations (1–3). As a powerful paradigm for empirical discovery, data-driven modeling has been widely applied to solve inverse problems in physics (4), uncover biological mechanisms (5–7), and enable precision diagnostics for complex diseases (8, 9). A key tool in these approaches is entropy, a fundamental concept in thermodynamics and information theory. Entropy provides a framework for modeling uncertainty, disorder, and irreversibility across a wide range of systems, from black holes (10, 11) and ecosystems (12) to digital infrastructures and quantum materials (13–15). According to the second law of thermodynamics, internal processes in closed systems are irreversible, leading to positive entropy production (12, 16). Thermodynamic principles have increasingly influenced deep learning: for instance, neural networks have been developed to construct DFTbased potential energy surfaces (17), Boltzmann Generators embed Boltzmann entropy to predict protein structures (18), and entropy-based frameworks like Thermodynamics-Inspired Explainable Representations and EPR-Net improve interpretability and modeling of high-dimensional non-equilibrium systems (19–21). Entropy-driven objectives—such as cross-entropy loss—have been widely applied in machine learning(22–25), particularly for classification tasks (26). However, traditional entropy-based methods typically provide only macroscopic or microscopic approximations and often fail to account for internal disparities when fusing multisource data. These limitations are particularly pronounced for real-world datasets, which vary in scale, resolution, measurement conditions, and statistical properties—posing challenges for conventional machine learning methods that assume data homogeneity and stationarity (27). To overcome these challenges, zentropy theory introduces a thermodynamically consistent framework that incorporates intrinsic entropy, capturing variability across datasets due to environmental, experimental, or contextual differences (28, 29). Originally developed to predict magnetic phase diagrams in fcc-Ce (30) and L12-Fe3Pt (31), zentropy has since been applied to a wide range of materials, including strongly correlated systems (32–34), liquid and melting phases (35), plasticity (36), and superconductivity (37). Despite its success in physics, zentropy’s reliance on DFT for computing configuration-level entropy has limited its application in broader data-driven domains. Nevertheless, as a foundational framework for integrating heterogeneous data, zentropy remains essential for ensuring thermodynamic consistency and enabling more accurate, comprehensive models. Building on this foundation, we introduce a zentropyenhanced neural network (ZENN) that embeds zentropy theory into deep learning to enable entropy-aware learning from complex, heterogeneous datasets. Unlike Shannon entropy, zentropy’s thermodynamic basis captures domainspecific complexity, providing richer structure for modeling multi-source data. By incorporating zentropy into neural network design, ZENN learns both energy and intrinsic entropy components, capturing the underlying structure of heterogeneous datasets. This approach extends the applicability of zentropy beyond quantum and statistical mechanics, offering a robust framework for integrating heterogeneous, real-world data and improving predictive performance in classification, energy landscape reconstruction, and other scientific applications. While zentropy has been widely used in forward modeling, where it plays a critical role in predicting system behavior, its integration into data-driven approaches for inverse problems remains unexplored. This paper addresses this gap by embedding zentropy theory into data science, advancing the application of entropy-based modeling within the context of data-driven techniques. More specifically, to broaden the applicability of zentropy theory beyond its traditional domain, we developed ZENN—a framework designed to integrate multi-source data, reconstruct energy landscapes, and infer system properties. We benchmarked ZENN against cross-entropy loss using state-of-the-art models for both image and text classification tasks and energy landscape reconstruction. In all cases, ZENN consistently outperformed cross-entropy loss in terms of accuracy and stability. To further validate its capabilities, ZENN was applied to the Fe3Pt compound, successfully capturing its negative thermal expansion (NTE) behavior and accurately predicting its critical point in the temperature-pressure/volume spaces. These results demonstrate ZENN’s strong generalization ability and its enhanced performance in estimating highorder derivatives—an essential feature for modeling complex thermodynamic systems
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
ZENN: Zentropy-Enhanced Neural Networks Bring Thermodynamic Principles to Machine Learning on Heterogeneous Data

News Article:  
A new neural network framework, known as the zentropy-enhanced neural network (ZENN), introduces a thermodynamically grounded architecture that fundamentally departs from conventional cross-entropy loss-based models by embedding intrinsic entropy into the learning process and introducing a learnable, dataset-aware ‘temperature’ parameter. This tunable temperature variable is central to ZENN’s approach, enabling the network to distinguish meaningful signal (energy) from irreducible uncertainty or noise (intrinsic entropy) within heterogeneous, multisource datasets—a feature absent from traditional neural networks. As recently highlighted by Science News, ZENN’s architecture is rooted in a thermodynamic split of input data, dynamically separating what is statistically significant from background uncertainty and using the temperature parameter to probe latent heterogeneity across disparate sources, ranging from high-fidelity simulations to noisy experimental measurements (Anchor Event: [3]). In doing so, ZENN offers a versatile solution for scientific machine learning tasks where conventional cross-entropy-based methods struggle with data variance, scale, and inconsistency.

This methodical integration of thermodynamics into deep learning marks a pronounced departure from the standard reliance on cross-entropy loss, which assumes tidy, homogeneous training data and lacks sensitivity to intrinsic disparities among data sources. ZENN’s design, conceived and advanced by Shun Wang, Wenrui Hao, Zi-Kui Liu, and Shunli Shang, is closely tied to Liu’s Zentropy theory. This theory merges foundational principles from quantum mechanics, thermodynamics, and statistical mechanics into a unified predictive framework, providing a coherent basis for discerning both macroscopic and fine-grained, configuration-level entropy in physical and data-driven systems alike ([Abstract], [Introduction]). By embedding zentropy theory directly into the neural architecture, ZENN is capable of simultaneously learning both energy and intrinsic entropy components, thus capturing the underlying structural heterogeneity that defines complex scientific datasets ([Abstract]).

The key innovation—the tunable temperature parameter—acts as a modulator for latent dataset variability, effectively calibrating the model’s response to source-specific disorder. In practical terms, this parameter enables ZENN to assign lower significance to high-noise samples (e.g., experimental data) and higher weight to more reliable sources (e.g., simulations), providing a physically consistent mechanism for integrating multisource information. According to the supporting literature, this allows ZENN to discern significant patterns and discount irrelevant noise not collectively or uniformly but source by source, making it well-suited for real-world datasets that vary in resolution, measurement protocols, and statistical bias ([Abstract], [Real-World Anchor Event], [Introduction]).

The technical implications of this advance are demonstrated across multiple benchmarks. In both image and text classification tasks—including CIFAR-10/100, BBC News, and AG News—ZENN’s thermodynamically inspired split, along with the temperature-adjusted intrinsic entropy modeling, yields superior generalization and robustness. The architecture repeatedly outperforms cross-entropy-based state-of-the-art models, particularly in scenarios involving complex, heterogeneous inputs ([Abstract]). Beyond standard classification, the ZENN framework extends to the reconstruction of high-order energy landscapes, as exemplified in the Helmholtz free energy profile of Fe3Pt, using data generated from density functional theory (DFT). In this context, ZENN accurately reconstructs key material behaviors, including negative thermal expansion and the location of a critical point in temperature–pressure space—demonstrating its ability to model and predict high-order derivatives and capture underlying physical properties not readily accessible to models lacking thermodynamic coherence ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

The underlying thermodynamic framework not only ensures improved generalization and stability for heterogeneous data but also positions ZENN as a tool for data-driven integration of real-time and historical measurements, simulations, and experimental datasets. Its architecture is particularly relevant for emergent domains such as digital twins, where continuous assimilation of disparate data sources underpins accurate, dynamic modeling of physical systems ([Introduction]). The model’s grounding in Zentropy theory, developed by Liu and extended by Wang, Hao, and Shang, ensures a rigorous basis for entropy-aware learning—a critical innovation as data heterogeneity and scale surpass the limits of conventional machine learning methods.

For researchers and practitioners in the fields of machine learning, computational physics, and materials science, ZENN offers a significant step forward in the systematic integration of thermodynamics into data science. By uniting the theoretical depth of quantum and statistical mechanics with neural architectures, it sets a precedent for future approaches in handling the uncertainty, variability, and complexity inherent in multisource datasets.

To explore the full methodological and experimental details, and to further review the implications of this thermodynamically enhanced approach to neural network design, access the research paper at https://dx.doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2511227122.

It offers a technically grounded, credible narrative outside the primary research, suitable as a compelling anchor.
anchor_event.full_content:
ZENN builds thermodynamics into neural networks. It separates what's meaningful in the data (energy) from what's uncertain or noisy (intrinsic entropy), and uses a tunable "temperature" parameter to account for hidden differences across datasets - for example, precise simulations versus noisier experiments.

Conventional neural networks often rely on cross-entropy loss and assume tidy, consistent training sets. That assumption breaks down with heterogeneous inputs. ZENN's thermodynamics-inspired split - signal (energy) versus uncertainty (intrinsic entropy) - plus a dataset-aware temperature term, lets the model pick out what matters and discount what doesn't, source by source.

The framework was developed by Shun Wang (mathematics), Wenrui Hao (mathematics; Center for Mathematical Biology), Zi-Kui Liu (materials science and engineering) and Shunli Shang (materials science and engineering). It is grounded in Liu's Zentropy theory - a deeper take on entropy that unifies quantum mechanics, thermodynamics and statistical mechanics into a predictive model.

Explicitly highlight the foundational involvement of Shun Wang, Wenrui Hao, Zi-Kui Liu, and Shunli Shang in developing ZENN, grounding its innovations firmly in Liu’s Zentropy theory, and emphasize the integration of quantum mechanics, thermodynamics, and statistical mechanics as the theoretical backbone of ZENN’s thermodynamic-inspired architecture.

title: New AI model accurately grades messy handwritten math answers and explains student errors
abstract: Automatically assessing handwritten mathe- matical solutions is an important problem in educational technology with practical ap- plications, but it remains a significant chal- lenge due to the diverse formats, unstruc- tured layouts, and symbolic complexity of stu- dent work. To address this challenge, we in- troduce VEHME-a Vision-Language Model for Evaluating Handwritten Mathematics Expressions—designed to assess open-form handwritten math responses with high accuracy and interpretable reasoning traces. VEHME integrates a two-phase training pipeline: (i) su- pervised fine-tuning using structured reason- ing data, and (ii) reinforcement learning that aligns model outputs with multi-dimensional grading objectives, including correctness, rea- soning depth, and error localization. To en- hance spatial understanding, we propose an Expression-Aware Visual Prompting Module, trained on our synthesized multi-line math ex- pressions dataset to robustly guide attention in visually heterogeneous inputs. Evaluated on AIHub and FERMAT datasets, VEHME achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models and approaches the accu- racy of proprietary systems, demonstrating its potential as a scalable and accessible tool for automated math assessment. Our training and experiment code is publicly available at our GitHub repository.

Introduction

evaluate student work to identify conceptual misun- derstandings, procedural errors, and gaps in logical reasoning. But, the scalability of this process is severely limited by time constraints, class sizes, and the cognitive load of interpreting diverse solu- tion strategies (Gowda and Suma, 2017; Laws et al., 2003; Callahan et al., 2021). With the rise of digi- tal learning platforms, there is an urgent need for automated systems that can replicate the nuanced judgment of human graders while accommodating the unstructured, creative, and often messy nature of student-written content (Baral et al., 2025; Scar- latos et al., 2025).

The assessment of handwritten mathematical solu- tions is a fundamental yet labor-intensive compo- nent of mathematics education, serving as both a diagnostic tool for educators and a critical feedback mechanism for learners (Scarlatos et al., 2025; Lin, 2024; Zhang et al., 2021; Nakamoto et al., 2023). In traditional classroom settings, teachers manually

Both authors contributed equally to this work. †Co-corresponding authors.

To address scalability challenges in grading, prior work has explored automated systems built around structured templates and Optical Charac- ter Recognition (OCR) pipelines (Li et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020, 2025; Wagstaff et al., 2019), as well as typed-text inputs processed by language models (Scarlatos et al., 2025). However, these approaches rely on rigid assumptions: that student responses are syntactically clean, spatially struc-

tured, or written within constrained templates. Fur- thermore, there is a notable lack of high-quality datasets that include complex, multi-expression handwritten math expressions (Yang et al., 2023), limiting the capacity to model open-form student solutions. Consequently, these systems often break down when applied to real-world data that is hand- written, unstructured, and heterogeneous, featuring symbolic math, diagrams, and natural language, all rendered in varied handwriting styles (Figure 1).

Previous approaches that leverage Large Lan- guage Models (LLMs) for grading often depend solely on the model’s end-to-end reasoning capa- bilities without explicitly supervising or constrain- ing the underlying reasoning process (Zhang et al., 2025; Mok et al., 2024; Kortemeyer et al., 2024). In the text-only domain, Process Reward Models (PRMs) have been introduced to address this issue by enabling step-by-step evaluation of mathemat- ical sequential reasoning (He et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024; Lambert et al., 2024). More recently, PRMs have been extended into the multimodal do- main (Khalifa et al., 2025; Wang et al., 2025), al- lowing reasoning-aware supervision in visual tasks. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is still no effective approach tailored to handwritten mathematical expressions, a domain that combines the complexities of symbolic reasoning with the challenges of visual noise and layout diversity.

While recent advances in Vision-Language Mod- els (VLMs) have shown promise in multimodal un- derstanding, their application to handwritten math assessment has been limited by several key chal- lenges: (C1) the scarcity of high-quality training data that pairs handwritten expressions with de- tailed error annotations (Jin et al., 2025), (C2) the difficulty in robustly processing the spatial lay- out and visual noise characteristic of student work (Guo et al., 2025b).

In this work, we take a step toward over- coming these challenges by tackling the under- explored and difficult task of grading template- free, handwritten open-response solutions in the broader domain of K–12 mathematics. This set- ting requires models capable of handling sig- nificant visual heterogeneity, unstructured lay- outs, and sequential reasoning, where early mis- takes may propagate across later steps. To this end, we present VEHME (a Vision-Language Model for Evaluating Handwritten Mathematics Expressions), a novel end-to-end framework for automatically grading handwritten math solutions.

Our key contributions are summarized as follows:

To address the challenge of assessing complex open-form mathematical expressions, we pro- pose a novel framework named VEHME. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to propose an end-to-end training pipeline for grading handwritten mathematical expres- sions.

We adopt a dual-phase training regime: super- vised fine-tuning on our synthesized datasets, followed by reinforcement learning with a composite reward to optimize for correctness, reasoning quality, and error localization.

To address C1, we also propose a data syn- thesis pipeline to construct a high-quality reasoning-augmented dataset that pairs hand- written expressions with error detection and localization reasoning traces.

To address C2, we propose the Expression- aware Visual Prompting Module (EVPM), equipping the VLM with precise spatial lo- calization of handwritten mathematical ex- pressions. To train this module, we construct a complex, heterogeneous, multi-expression handwritten math expressions dataset with its corresponding spatial information.

Our training pipeline enables small, open- source Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to surpass the performance of larger open-source counterparts and achieve results on par with state-of-the-art proprietary models.

Headline:  
Korean Researchers Unveil VEHME: An Open-Source AI Model for Human-Like Grading of Messy Handwritten Math

News Article:  
Korean researchers at UNIST and POSTECH have introduced VEHME, an open-source Vision-Language Model capable of grading handwritten math solutions with human-like precision—even when the writing is crooked or visually unstructured (source: [Search Result 3](https://en.sedaily.com/technology/2025/12/17/korean-researchers-develop-ai-that-grades-messy-handwritten)). This breakthrough directly addresses one of education's longstanding bottlenecks: the reliable, automated assessment of open-ended, handwritten mathematics in real classrooms, where student solutions often diverge wildly in layout, symbolic complexity, and handwriting style.

The technical significance of VEHME arises from its integration of multimodal analysis techniques tailored to the unique demands of mathematical expression assessment—an area where conventional Optical Character Recognition (OCR) pipelines, rule-based templates, or language-model-based approaches have consistently failed to scale. Existing solutions are generally predicated on syntactically clean or spatially structured inputs, a constraint consistently violated in real-world educational contexts. As detailed in the VEHME paper ([Citation](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2510.22798)), most prior models break down in the presence of visual noise, heterogeneous layouts, symbolic notation, diagrams, and the cross-modal logical chains inherent to student-written mathematics.

VEHME directly targets these technical challenges through several key architectural advances. Central to its efficacy is the Expression-Aware Visual Prompting Module (EVPM), a proprietary visual conditioning component. The EVPM enables VEHME to perform robust spatial localization of handwritten mathematical expressions, handling the intricacies of multi-line solutions and guiding model attention even amidst visually heterogeneous, open-form answers. As confirmed in both the official abstract and introduction, EVPM is trained on a synthesized dataset of complex, multi-expression handwritten math, with explicit spatial cues constructed to improve the model’s ability to track the order and semantic context of variable solution steps.

VEHME's reasoning and grading capabilities are further enhanced via a two-phase training pipeline. The first phase employs supervised fine-tuning, utilizing structured reasoning data augmented with explicit error localization and detection traces—effectively teaching the model to discriminate between correctness, conceptual misunderstandings, procedural breakdowns, and the specific logical points of failure within a student’s work. The second phase leverages reinforcement learning with a composite, multi-dimensional reward: this process aligns model outputs not only with the objective of correctness, but also with reasoning quality and granular error localization. The dual-phase approach ensures that VEHME's evaluation is not only state-consistent (verifying final answers), but also process-consistent (tracing through intermediate steps and isolating missteps in reasoning or algebraic manipulation).

This architectural orchestration enables VEHME to resolve two previously unaddressed impediments in automated math grading: (C1) the lack of high-quality, reasoning-augmented paired datasets for handwritten mathematics (addressed via the authors’ custom data synthesis pipeline), and (C2) the challenge of robustly processing spatially complex, visually noisy student solutions (solved by EVPM integration, per the introduction). The result, substantiated in the VEHME evaluation on the AIHub and FERMAT datasets, is state-of-the-art performance among open-source models—approaching the accuracy benchmarks of proprietary systems as reported in the real-world anchor event.

A second-order technical implication, with direct relevance to the research community, is VEHME's demonstrated ability to perform error localization. Beyond mere correctness grading, the model can identify and annotate error locations, providing error category traces (e.g., in fraction manipulation, equation setup, or logical progression), thereby enabling scalable feedback loops for both educators and automated tutoring systems. This aligns with the authors’ objective to model not just “end-to-end” solution assessment, but nuanced, interpretable reasoning chains—a crucial distinction from prior language-only or vision-only automated grading efforts.

Notably, VEHME accomplishes these advances using a training pipeline that allows small, open-source Vision-Language Models to outperform larger open-source alternatives, thereby lowering the barrier to adoption and experimentation in research and education communities.

This release marks a substantive step towards democratizing access to interpretable, high-accuracy assessment tools for mathematical education. VEHME’s methodologies and datasets can now be freely examined, benchmarked, and extended by domain experts seeking to address the evolving challenges of STEM assessment.

For technical readers interested in implementation details, evaluation results, or contributing to future iterations, the authors’ codebase and experimental documentation are publicly available. Access the full research paper and repository at the provided [GitHub link](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2510.22798) to interrogate the methodology and explore potential integration pathways within your own research or learning environments.

Markdown Content:
Korean researchers have developed an AI model capable of grading handwritten math answers with human-like precision, even when the writing is crooked or messy.

Grading open-ended math problems is one of the most time-consuming tasks in education, yet automating it has proven difficult. Math solutions typically contain a mix of equations, graphs and figures, with each student's handwriting and answer layout varying significantly, making it challenging for AI to accurately recognize and identify errors.

The research team built VEHME using a proprietary technology called Expression-aware Visual Prompt Module (EVPM) and a two-stage learning technique. EVPM allows VEHME to place virtual boxes around complex sequences of equations so it does not lose track of the solution order. The two-stage reinforcement learning enables the model to explain not just whether an answer is correct, but also which part of the solution is wrong and why.

title: Helping AI agents search to get the best results out of large language models
abstract: We introduce a new approach to agent programming, the development of LLM- based agents. Current approaches to agent programming often entangle two aspects of agent design: the core workflow logic and the inference-time strategy (e.g., tree search). We introduce probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN), a programming model that disentangles these two concerns, allowing the programmer to describe the agent workflow and independently experiment with different inference-time strategies by simply changing a few inputs. We provide an implementation of PAN in Python as the ENCOMPASS framework, which uses a Python decorator to compile agent workflow programs into a search space. We present three case studies that demonstrate how the framework lets the programmer quickly improve the reliability of an agent and easily switch between different inference-time strategies, all with little additional coding.
introduction:
Introduction

Recent work has shown the power of scaling inference-time compute for LLMs [1, 2], where popular strategies include best-of-N sampling [3, 4, 5], refinement [6, 7], and tree search [8, 9]. In LLM-based agents — systems that define how LLMs and other components interact to solve a task — these same strategies have become common ways of improving performance and reliability. Furthermore, several works have demonstrated the utility of applying sophisticated search and backtracking strategies in AI agents to improve performance in various tasks [8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14].

While various frameworks have been developed to simplify the low-level interaction between the program and the LLM [15, 16, 17, 18], a framework for agent inference-time strategies has been absent. Our goal is to develop an inference-time strategy framework: a framework that makes it easy to experiment with different inference-time strategies independently of the design and implementation of the underlying agent workflow. Such a framework is intended not to replace, but to be used in conjunction with LLM prompting and tool use frameworks, such as LangChain [15] or DSPy [16].

We target “program-in-control” style agents, where one defines the workflow in code and uses the LLM to accomplish specific subtasks [14, 19, 20, 21, 22].2 In these agents, inference-time strategies have traditionally been limited to sampling and refinement loops [6, 7, 19, 20], whereas more sophisticated strategies such as beam search and tree search have been rarely explored [14].

∗Work performed as a consultant for Asari AI 2This “program-in-control” style contrasts with the “LLM-in-control” style where the LLM decides the full

sequence of operations (tool calls) in the workflow [8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13].

Figure 1: An ENCOMPASS program specifies an agent workflow, which is compiled into a search space object, and inference-time scaling is accomplished through search over the nondeterministic execution paths of the agent workflow.

We identify the key bottleneck to be the entanglement of the inference-time scaling strategy with the core workflow logic when programming the agent. Programmers typically bake the inference- time strategy into the agent workflow [14, 19, 20, 22], which is inflexible, reduces readability, and limits the kinds of inference-time strategies that can be easily implemented. Therefore, we aim to design a framework that cleanly separates the representation of the core workflow logic from the inference-time scaling strategy. The programmer could then make minimal modifications to their agent to flexibly experiment with different inference-time strategies. Also, different agents would no longer require custom implementations of the same inference-time strategy, but can instead reuse a common implementation.

Our key insight is that inference-time strategies can be viewed as instances of search over different execution paths of a nondeterministic program. We developed the ENCOMPASS Python programming framework (“enhancing agents with compiled agent search”), depicted in Figure 1. Figure 1a and Figure 1b show an agent program and its corresponding workflow, respectively. The user specifies the “locations of unreliability” in their agent source code using branchpoint() statements. A location of unreliability is an operation such as an LLM call where repeated invocations produce outputs of varying quality. Since these different outputs give rise to multiple possible futures of the program’s execution, the program has a tree of possible execution paths. ENCOMPASS compiles the program into a search space object (Figure 1c) so that search can be conducted over this tree of execution paths to find the path with the highest score (Figure 1d). We call this programming model probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). As a form of angelic nondeterminism [23], PAN lets the programmer write their program pretending the unreliable operations always produce good outputs, and the runtime searches the space of possible execution paths for one where the operations indeed produced good outputs.

Our work makes the following concrete contributions:

We introduce the PAN programming model (Section 2.1), which uses angelic nondetermin- ism to separate inference-time algorithms (search policy) from the underlying logic of the agent (specification of the search space).

We present ENCOMPASS, a Python library that implements PAN (Section 2.2), providing 1. primitives like branchpoint() that the programmer can use inside their ENCOMPASS function, 2. a Python function decorator that compiles an ENCOMPASS function into a search space object at run-time, and 3. common search algorithms, as well as an interface for implementing custom search algorithms.

We illustrate how ENCOMPASS provides a unifying framework for common inference-time strategies and agentic patterns, which are special cases of search over nondeterministic execution paths of ENCOMPASS programs (Section 3). ENCOMPASS also provides a natural generalization of these inference-time strategies.

We present case studies showing how ENCOMPASS enables easy experimentation of various inference-time search strategies over an underlying agent workflow, allowing one to quickly identify the best-performing strategy (Section 4). ENCOMPASS opens up new possibilities for inference-time scaling of program-in-control style agents, where inference-time strategies that were previously considered too cumbersome to implement are now made possible by ENCOMPASS.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
ENCOMPASS Framework Redefines AI Agent Programming with Structured Search and Probabilistic Angelic Nondeterminism

News Article:  
The emergence of the ENCOMPASS framework signifies a critical inflection point in the integration of AI-driven agents and search-based techniques into software engineering workflows. As underscored by Carnegie Mellon University Professor Yiming Yang in recent MIT News coverage, ENCOMPASS enables a principled separation of agent programming logic and inference-time search strategies. This abstraction, according to Yang, “provides a solid foundation for more systematic and reliable search-driven approaches to software development,” addressing a long-standing technical bottleneck within the field.

At the core of ENCOMPASS is its implementation of probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN), a programming model formalized to untangle inference-time strategy from core workflow logic in the construction of LLM-based agents ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]). Traditionally, AI agent programming in the “program-in-control” style—where deterministic workflow code orchestrates LLM invocations for subtasks—has led to rigid, entangled implementations. This approach complicates the iterative experimentation with sophisticated search algorithms vital for improving agent performance and reliability.

ENCOMPASS advances the state-of-the-art by treating inference-time strategies as search problems over nondeterministic program execution paths. This is achieved by enabling programmers to explicitly annotate “locations of unreliability”—such as LLM outputs with variable quality—using branchpoint() primitives within their ENCOMPASS functions. The ENCOMPASS Python decorator then compiles the specified agent workflow into a search space object, converting the nondeterministic agent into a tree of possible executions. In this paradigm, inference-time scaling is realized by overlaying structured search—such as beam search or backtracking—over the compiled space of execution pathways, as presented in Figure 1 of the original work ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

This methodology yields multiple technical advantages. First, it separates inference-time search policy (the choice and tuning of search algorithms at runtime) from the agent’s workflow specification. As a consequence, developers can experiment with, benchmark, and improve upon inference-time strategies with minimal changes to source code ([Abstract], [Introduction]). Zhening Li, lead author and PhD student at MIT EECS and CSAIL, affirms this point: “Our framework lets programmers easily experiment with different search strategies to find the one that makes the AI agent perform the best” ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

Empirical evidence substantiates ENCOMPASS’s technical contributions. In documented case studies, the framework was applied to LLM-driven agents tasked with translating large repositories from Java to Python. This scenario highlights the substantial code complexity reduction enabled by ENCOMPASS: by employing branchpoint annotations and structured search overlays, the implementation required 348 fewer lines of code—an 82 percent reduction compared to ad-hoc solutions ([Real-World Anchor Event]). Furthermore, ENCOMPASS permitted seamless experimentation with alternative inference-time strategies, ultimately identifying a two-level beam search as optimal for the translation task. This approach delivered a measured accuracy increase of 15 to 40 percent across five distinct repositories, with a controlled search budget set at 16 times the baseline number of LLM calls ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

Notably, the adoption of ENCOMPASS obviates the historically laborious process of custom engineering inference-time search per agent. The framework exposes unified primitives and runtime interfaces for common and custom search algorithms, encapsulating the search-driven agentic patterns recognized in contemporary LLM literature (e.g., best-of-N sampling, refinement, and tree search) ([Abstract], [Introduction]). This architectural decoupling enables rapid, reproducible evaluation of workflow and search strategy combinations within existing agent pipelines and is fully compatible with popular LLM developer frameworks such as LangChain and DSPy.

ENCOMPASS does not intend to supplant the wider LLM tool and prompt ecosystem; rather, it introduces an orthogonal control layer that enhances the robustness, reliability, and statistical power of program-in-control agents by leveraging the theory and practice of search over nondeterministic execution. Its design generalizes and systematizes inference-time scaling for agent pipelines, making previously burdensome search mechanisms accessible for both foundational research and industrial application ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

To deepen your understanding of probabilistic angelic nondeterminism and the architectural implications of ENCOMPASS for search-driven agent programming, readers are encouraged to access the full research paper at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.03571.

This reporting confirms independent recognition from a quality source, summarizing ENCOMPASS's practical impact and technical innovation in workflow-search separation.
anchor_event.full_content:
“EnCompass arrives at a timely moment, as AI-driven agents and search-based techniques are beginning to reshape workflows in software engineering,” says Carnegie Mellon University Professor Yiming Yang, who wasn’t involved in the research. “By cleanly separating an agent’s programming logic from its inference-time search strategy, the framework offers a principled way to explore how structured search can enhance code generation, translation, and analysis. This abstraction provides a solid foundation for more systematic and reliable search-driven approaches to software development.”

“With EnCompass, we’ve separated the search strategy from the underlying workflow of an AI agent,” says lead author Zhening Li ’25, MEng ’25, who is an MIT electrical engineering and computer science (EECS) PhD student, CSAIL researcher, and research consultant at Asari AI. “Our framework lets programmers easily experiment with different search strategies to find the one that makes the AI agent perform the best.”

For example, the researchers applied EnCompass to an agent that translates a repository of code from the Java programming language, which is commonly used to program apps and enterprise software, to Python. They found that implementing search with EnCompass — mainly involving adding branchpoint annotations and annotations that record how well each step did — required 348 fewer lines of code (about 82 percent) than implementing it by hand. They also demonstrated how EnCompass enabled them to easily try out different search strategies, identifying the best strategy to be a two-level beam search algorithm, achieving an accuracy boost of 15 to 40 percent across five different repositories at a search budget of 16 times the LLM calls made by the agent without search.

BRIDGE CONSTRAINT: The article must directly connect the technical anchor—the separation of agent workflow logic from inference-time search strategies via probabilistic angelic nondeterminism in ENCOMPASS—to the insights and evidence provided in the retrieved content. This includes detailing how ENCOMPASS enables experimentation with different search strategies (citing Zhening Li), reporting the reduction in code implementation complexity (348 fewer lines), and demonstrating increased accuracy (15 to 40 percent) through structured search (e.g., two-level beam search) in real-world code translation tasks, all while reinforcing that these findings enable systematic, reliable, and scalable search-driven approaches in software development.
