Headline:  
Debate-Driven AI: How Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) Advances Factual Accuracy and Reasoning in Large Language Models

News Article:  
Simulating internal debates among AI agents with distinct personalities has recently been shown to drive sharper decision-making and improved reliability in large language models (LLMs). A confluence of research from leading AI labs, including Google, demonstrates that models equipped to participate in multi-agent, role-diverse debate scenarios—where each agent critiques and defends hypotheses—outperform counterparts trained on isolated monologues. This paradigm shift is technically anchored by the Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) framework, which leverages agent diversity and dynamic coordination to boost factual accuracy and logical sophistication in LLM outputs (Abstract; Real-World Anchor Event).

Conventional LLMs—while prolific in generating coherent text and reasoning chains—have been dogged by persistent issues in factual reliability and logical consistency. These shortcomings stem from their exposure to noisy training data and the absence of explicit mechanisms for reasoning verification during standard next-token prediction (Introduction). Despite advances in prompting (e.g., chain-of-thought, self-consistency), tool-use augmentation, reinforcement learning from human feedback, and self-reflective post-hoc refinements, the dominant paradigm has remained single-model and static (Introduction). This architecture is fundamentally limited: it cannot exploit the diverse perspectives necessary to correct for model-specific blind spots or logical omissions.

Empirical breakthroughs now indicate that multi-agent debate—especially with heterogeneous, role-specific agents—outperforms these legacy approaches. For example, recent industry and academic studies reveal that language models fine-tuned on multi-agent debate conversational data achieve markedly higher accuracy and exhibit faster improvement in reasoning than those trained on monologic data streams (Real-World Anchor Event). This approach, described as 'society of thought', leverages specialized agent interactions to refine argumentation and error-checking. Extending this insight, A-HMAD formalizes the multi-agent debate process: it organizes a set of LLM agents, each with a distinct specialization (such as logical reasoning, factual verification, or strategic planning), into a coordinated, adaptive ensemble (Abstract).

The technical innovation underpinning A-HMAD lies in its threefold approach (Abstract; Introduction): first, the system instantiates a heterogeneous ensemble of agents, each configured with unique roles or even separate model types. This ensures that perspectives on a given problem are not only multiplied but diversified, allowing the ensemble to catch errors that homogeneous agent pools systematically overlook. Second, A-HMAD implements dynamic debate routing via a learned coordination policy, selectively activating subsets of agents based on the question domain and the intermediate state of the argument. This dynamic routing directly contrasts with the rigid, turn-based debate protocols of prior art. Third, the consensus among agents is determined not by simple majority vote, but by a learned consensus optimizer that adaptively weights each agent’s contribution according to both statistical reliability and self-assessed confidence. This mechanism allows A-HMAD to place more credence on expertise relevant to the task at hand, further elevating final answer quality.

These innovations are substantiated by rigorous benchmarking. On six challenging datasets—including arithmetic QA, GSM8K grade-school math, MMLU multifact QA, factual biography generation, and chess strategy—A-HMAD consistently outperforms both single-model techniques and prior homogeneous multi-agent debate baselines (Abstract). Notably, this translates into absolute accuracy gains of 4–6% over standard debate approaches and over 30% reductions in factual errors on tasks like biography generation (Abstract). Such quantitative improvements directly mirror those observed in the real-world anchor studies, where models simulating societal debate registered analogous leaps in reliability and complex reasoning (Real-World Anchor Event).

A-HMAD’s structural choices are rigorously probed through ablation studies, confirming the independent and cumulative benefits of agent heterogeneity, increased debate rounds, and the learned consensus module (Abstract). Furthermore, analyses indicate that specialist agents not only identify and correct one another’s logical missteps but also bring unique, domain-resonant scrutiny that homogenized models cannot deliver (Introduction).

A-HMAD demonstrates that structured, adaptive, and diverse agent debate is technically superior for driving factual accuracy and logical robustness in large language models. The framework exemplifies how anchoring LLMs in a 'society of minds'—wherein adjudicated debate supersedes monologic output—propels reliability, interpretability, and pedagogical soundness in AI systems.

Researchers and practitioners seeking to mitigate error propagation and bias in AI-driven reasoning tasks should consider the Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate approach. For a comprehensive treatment of its methodological underpinnings, empirical results, and robust ablation analyses, access the full research article via https://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00353-3.

title: New 3D benchmark leaves AI in knots
abstract:
We propose KNOTGYM, an interactive environment for complex, spatial reasoning and manipulation. KNOTGYM includes goal-oriented rope manipulation tasks with varying levels of complexity, all requiring acting from pure image observations. Tasks are defined along a clear and quantifiable axis of complexity based on the number of knot crossings, creating a natural generalization test. KNOTGYM has a simple observation space, allowing for scalable development, yet it highlights core challenges in integrating acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation. We evaluate methods of different classes, including model-based RL, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning, and illustrate the challenges KNOTGYM presents. KNOTGYM is available at https://github. com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Figure 1: KNOTGYM is a visual reasoning knot manipulation environment. It includes three tasks: transforming complex knots to a simple loop (example in the center); tying a loop into complex knots (right pane); and converting one knot into another, given a goal knot image. KNOTGYM has a continuous visual observation space (left pane) and an action space of applying forces to contact points (right pane), abstracting the specifics of robot end effectors. The space of goals, specified using Gauss code, is a factorial of the number of crossings (#X), which creates a ladder of generalization. Each goal defines an easily testable equivalence class over a continuous set of states.
introduction:
Introduction

KNOTGYM is a knot manipulation environment to train and evaluate visual reasoning agents. Figure 1 illustrates KNOTGYM. The agent observes the world state as an image and needs to transform a knot into a topological goal by applying forces to rope segments. The goal is communicated to the agent by observing a secondary knot as an exemplar. KNOTGYM defines three tasks (Table 1), each characterized by its start state and goal. KNOTGYM is designed with several aspects in mind:

Research Challenges KNOTGYM evaluates spatial reasoning, action prediction, planning, and abstraction skills in a single environment. Solving a task requires analyzing the current rope configura- tion, planning on deforming it to achieve the goal, and mapping this plan to a sequence of continuous action predictions. Critically, KNOTGYM does not enforce one specific state as the goal – the goal is a large equivalence class of states. Goals are defined via Gauss code, a formal mathematical description of knot configuration based on its crossings. When the goal is communicated using an exemplar image, solving a task requires abstracting over the exemplar to identify the right set of actions to complete the task. We show that KNOTGYM is a significant challenge for contemporary methods.

Measurable Complexity Knots are well studied in mathematics, with formal descriptions like Gauss code and a measure of complexity in the number of crossings.1 Figure 1 shows various knot configurations with different numbers of crossings, illustrating the increasing complexity of more crossings. This measure of complexity enables to control the challenges of learning and evaluation.

Generalization Ladder The number of crossings creates a clear generalization ladder. This allows a clear train-test complexity split, where we train up to a certain level of complexity (i.e., number of crossings) and test if a model generalizes beyond this number. During training, this allows us to experiment with a natural learning curriculum, where the number of crossings in training examples is gradually increased. It also enables the study of self-improving bootstrapping processes in a visual domain, as recently proposed for arithmetic and maze-solving [Lee et al., 2025b].

Research Accessibility KNOTGYM is an accessible task particularly suited for studying extended visual reasoning in a laboratory environment, just as Pendulum for classic control and Countdown for reasoning in natural language [Gandhi et al., 2024]. We follow the implementation and design principles of OpenAI Gym [Brockman et al., 2016] to make the KNOTGYM as accessible for researchers as possible. This includes following the standard Gymnasium API [Towers et al., 2024] and supporting vectorized environments on multiple CPUs. The KNOTGYM environment and our baselines are available under the MIT license at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Table 1: The three tasks of KNOTGYM. We show the final observation assuming the goal is achieved. Correctly completing tasks does not require getting the exact exemplar configuration (right rope in each image pair), but a configuration with the same Gauss code, a notation that describes the abstract spatial characteristics of a knot. For example, the final observations in the tie row are both [1+,1-,2+,2-], despite their different appearances.

Task name

Description

Initial→ Final Observations

unknot

Untangle a knot into a simple loop

tie

Tie a goal knot from a simple loop

convert

Tie a new knot from an old knot

1While the number of crossings is a fitting measure of complexity to our manipulation task, the prime complexity measure of interest in mathematics is more likely the counting of crossing of irreducible knots (i.e., as part of the tabulation of prime knots). Under this perspective, all the knots in Figure 1 are equivalent, because they all can be reduced to a simple loop by applying a few (or many) Reidemeister moves. For us, the number of crossings is a more appropriate measure because we aim for manipulation and observation complexity.

Headline:  
KNOTGYM Introduces a Standardized Benchmark for Image-Based Knot Manipulation: Advancing Embodied AI Evaluation Beyond ManipBench

News Article:  
KNOTGYM has been introduced as a standardized, image-based environment for evaluating AI agent capabilities on complex knot-tying and untying tasks, representing a timely response to the critical need for robust benchmarks in AI-driven robotics and manipulation. This launch occurs amidst growing momentum in the community, as seen with the release of benchmarks like ManipBench, which rigorously assess the reasoning abilities of vision-language models (VLMs) for low-level robotic manipulation through large-scale, real-world task sets and correlation-based evaluation methodologies. The introduction of KNOTGYM directly addresses the call—evident across ManipBench and related efforts—for benchmark environments that challenge and standardize the measurement of embodied spatial reasoning and manipulation in AI agents.

KNOTGYM’s contribution is situated within this evolving landscape of standardized embodied AI evaluation. As detailed in its documentation, KNOTGYM offers a controlled, interactive environment where agents act from pure image observations to solve rope manipulation tasks of varying complexity, defined along a quantifiable axis—specifically, the number of knot crossings. This focus establishes a natural "generalization ladder," enabling systematic curriculum design and generalization testing, which has become a central research design in cutting-edge benchmarks.

To contextualize KNOTGYM’s technical significance and situate it among current benchmarks in the domain, it is instructive to compare its approach and scope with ManipBench, as well as with benchmarks such as NEWTON, PhysBench, VLABench, MultiNet, and Octopi. ManipBench, for example, operationalizes the benchmarking of VLMs by assembling an extensive set of multiple-choice questions covering a wide array of real-world robotic tasks, particularly in low-level manipulation. ManipBench’s methodology includes comprehensive statistical correlation analyses—most saliently, demonstrating strong linear and monotonic alignment between MCQ benchmark performance and real-world robotic effectiveness, thereby validating the benchmark’s proxy utility for embodied agent evaluation.

In contrast, KNOTGYM diverges from ManipBench's question-answer format by embedding agents within a continuous visual control environment where they must transform ropes from one knot configuration to another, relying solely on image-based observation and goal exemplars. Task achievement in KNOTGYM does not require exact replication of an exemplar configuration, but instead the realization of any configuration within the same Gauss code equivalence class—formalizing knot configuration based on its crossings. This abstraction bridges perceptual generalization and manipulative planning, foregrounding the integration of acute visual reasoning, spatial abstraction, and grounded manipulation.

While prior benchmarks such as PhysBench and NEWTON primarily focus on multiple-choice spatial or physical reasoning tasks, and VLABench and MultiNet assess the prediction of object properties and trajectories, KNOTGYM introduces a manipulation challenge centered on a mathematically tractable and incrementally complex domain: knot manipulation. Unlike benchmarks depending on skill libraries or static property prediction, KNOTGYM operates under the OpenAI Gymnasium API, facilitating vectorized training and evaluation on multiple CPUs. This design choice supports scalable experimentation, mirroring accessibility standards set by earlier environments like Pendulum for control and Countdown for natural language reasoning.

The technical novelty of KNOTGYM lies in its three core tasks—unknot (untangling complex knots into simple loops), tie (tying a loop into a complex knot), and convert (transforming one knot into another, guided by a goal image). By parameterizing task complexity through the number of crossings—a measure grounded in mathematical knot theory but specifically tailored here for manipulation and observation constraints—KNOTGYM both challenges existing agent architectures and provides stratified quantitative evaluation. The explicit use of Gauss code as an abstract, formal means of goal specification further distinguishes KNOTGYM’s approach from benchmarks like ManipBench, which instead rely on task decomposition and answer sets for evaluation.

In summary, KNOTGYM directly extends the domain of embodied spatial reasoning and robot manipulation benchmarks by providing a mathematically well-structured, visually grounded platform for acute perception, planning, and action in the context of knot manipulation. This complements, and crucially, broadens the scope set forth by ManipBench and similar benchmarks, ushering in a new level of granularity and standardization for embodied AI evaluation.

Researchers are encouraged to access KNOTGYM and the baseline evaluations, which are freely available under the MIT license, to further explore and advance methodologies for visual reasoning, manipulation, and embodied AI. Full technical details, source code, and baseline agent implementations can be found at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym and in the paper at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.18028.

ManipBench is technically deep, with a substantial question set and coverage of low-level manipulation tasks, reflecting advanced evaluation standards in this research space.
anchor_event.full_content:
Benchmarking LLMs and VLMs. Alongside the rapid advances in LLMs and VLMs, significant work has focused on benchmarking these models. Popular benchmarks evaluate mathematical rea-soning [38, 39], trust and safety [40], and visual tasks like interpreting charts and maps [41]. Others, such as AgentBench [42], assess LLMs as agents in code, game, and web environments. In con-trast, our benchmark centers on robotics, complementing existing efforts in VLM-based mapping and navigation [43], compositional reasoning [44], and task planning for embodied AI [45, 46, 47]. 2Low-level Manip. Reasoning Manip. Task Factorization Perf. Trans. Eval. Real World Data Fabric Manipulation Dynamic Manipulation Model Diversity PhysBench [9] ✗ ✗ ✓ ✓ ✗ ✗ High NEWTON [8] ✗ ✗ ✗ ✗ ✗ ✗ Medium VLABench [11] ✓ ✓ ✓ ✗ ✗ ✗ Low PhysObjects* [12] ✗ ✗ N/A ✓ ✗ ✗ N/A MultiNet [13] ✓ ✗ ✗ ✓ ✓ ✓ Low Octopi* [10] ✗ ✗ N/A ✓ ✗ ✗ N/A Open6DOR [14] ✓ ✓ ✗ ✓ ✗ ✗ Medium ETPBench [15] ✗ ✓ ✗ ✗ ✗ ✗ Medium GemBench* [16] ✓ ✗ N/A ✗ ✗ ✗ N/A ManipBench ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ High

To demonstrate the utility of ManipBench, we evaluate a set of selected VLMs on 7 unseen real world manipulation tasks (Sec. 6). We perform statistical analysis to assess the correlation between VLM performance on our MCQ benchmark and their effectiveness as real-world robotic agents (Table. 4). On aggregating the accuracies over the different question-types, we observe a Pearson’s coefficient of 0.889 ( p = 0 .003 ), a Spearman’s coefficient of 0.850 ( p = 0 .007 ), and a Kendall’s Tau of 0.691 ( p = 0 .018 ). These results indicate a strong positive correlation between MCQ benchmark performance and real-world effectiveness. The statistically significant ( p < 0.05 ) Pearson’s coeffi-cient highlights strong linear alignment, while the Spearman and Kendall coefficients confirm robust monotonic agreement with high confidence in rank-order consistency. Collectively, these trends sup-port the utility of ManipBench as a reliable proxy for evaluating VLMs in embodied robotic settings. See Appendix H for details on correlations for each question type with the real-world experiments.

More closely-related benchmarks, such as NEWTON [8] and Octopi [10], evaluate physical rea-soning capabilities. Here, VLMs answer multiple choice questions about object properties (such as whether an object is “brittle” or “soft”) from language and, for Octopi, high-resolution tactile data. Recently, the PhysBench [9] benchmark evaluates VLMs using multiple choice questions to test spatial reasoning capabilities. In contrast, ManipBench does not involve explicitly predicting object properties, and it evaluates how well VLMs can directly predict keypoints which define a low-level action for a robot. Other benchmarks for VLMs and robot manipulation include MultiNet [13] and VLABench [11]. MultiNet uses data from Open-X [48] and assesses how well VLMs predict tra-jectories using mean square error (MSE). However, using MSE for evaluation may not adequately measure performance in the case of multimodality. VLABench evaluates VLMs on manipulation tasks and assumes the presence of a skill library, whereas we do not use a skill library. In contrast to prior benchmarks, ManipBench also has a much greater focus on the important topic of deformable object manipulation [49, 50]. See Table 1 for an overview comparison.

title: How AI helps solve problems it doesnt even understand
abstract: Streamlining constraints (or streamliners, for short) narrow the search space, enhancing the speed and feasibility of solving complex constraint satisfaction problems. Traditionally, streamliners were crafted manually or generated through systematically combined atomic constraints with high-effort offline testing. Our approach utilizes the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to propose effective streamliners for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language and integrates feedback to the LLM with quick empirical tests for validation. Evaluated across seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, our method achieves substantial runtime reductions. We compare the results to obfuscated and disguised variants of the problem to see whether the results depend on LLM memorization. We also analyze whether longer offline runs improve the quality of streamliners and whether the LLM can propose good combinations of streamliners.
introduction:
Introduction

Streamliners are certain constraints added to a constraint model to reduce the search space, thereby improving the feasibility and speed of finding solutions to complex constraint satisfac- tion problems. By incorporating domain-specific knowledge, streamliners can guide the con- straint solver, allowing it to bypass less promising areas of the search space. Gomes and Sell- mann [2004] introduced streamliners to speed up the constrained-based search for hard combina- torial design problems. Today, streamliners are a standard tool for speeding up constrained-based search. Streamliners are closely related to implied/redundant constraints, symmetry-breaking constraints, and dominance-breaking constraints; however, adding a streamliner may even cause the constraint model to become inconsistent.

Originally, streamliners were hand-crafted by researchers who used their theoretical insight to analyze the constrained model. However, progress has also been made on the automated gen- eration of streamliners [Spracklen et al., 2023] by systematically trying the effect of some atomic constraints, such as imposing specific constraints on integer and function domains, like enforcing odd or even values, monotonicity, and properties like commutativity, as well as facilitating spe- cific attributes in binary relations. These atomic restrictions are tested on thousands of problem instances, and those that show a good streamlining effect are systematically combined.

This paper proposes a different approach to automated streamliner generation that utilizes the generative capabilities (“creativity”) of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate stream- liners, providing a neuro-symbolic prototype implementation StreamLLM, and rigorously tests

it on seven different constraint satisfaction problems using at least 310 CPU days1. Our ap- proach leverages the capabilities of LLMs to infer potentially effective streamliners, similar to an experienced researcher’s intuitive grasp of a problem. By integrating LLMs into the stream- liner generation process, we can potentially uncover unique and subtle patterns in the problem formulation and utilize them to speed up the solving process. To some extent, StreamLLM is closer related to a hand-crafted streamliner design by human experts rather than an automated bottom-up streamliner generation, the dominant strategy of the previous research [Spracklen et al., 2023].

Our system StreamLLM for streamliner generation combines queries to LLMs with quick em- pirical validation on small test instances solvable within seconds. The system generates effective constraints within minutes through engineered prompts and adaptive feedback based on stream- liner performance. This realtime approach allows streamliner benefits to be realized even for hard problem instances that would typically require hours to solve—any significant speedup can outweigh the brief time spent on streamliner generation.

We evaluate StreamLLM extensively across seven diverse constraint problems, including stan- dard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, using hundreds of instances spec- ified in MiniZinc. The problems are formulated in the MiniZinc constraint programming lan- guage [Nethercote et al., 2007]. We test StreamLLM with two state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet), different prompting variants, and different strategies for combining adaptive prompting with test runs on easy instances. Our results demonstrate remarkable effectiveness, with some streamliners reducing solving time by up to 99%.

To investigate whether these improvements stem from memorization or genuine problem un- derstanding, we test StreamLLM on disguised and obfuscated variants of the problems. The system’s continued strong performance, particularly on problems with no known streamliners in the literature, suggests real analytical capabilities rather than mere pattern matching.

Beyond the realtime setting, we explore offline approaches with extended training periods and larger instance sets, achieving even better results in some cases. We also investigate StreamLLM’s ability to generate effective combinations of streamliners, finding that this often outperforms single constraints. Analysis of the generated streamliners reveals an interesting mix—some mirror expert-designed constraints while others take novel yet highly effective approaches.

Our experimental results suggest that LLM-generated streamliners can meaningfully reduce runtime across diverse constraint satisfaction problems with minimal problem-specific tuning, pointing to promising applications of AI assistance in constraint programming for computationally challenging problems.

Part of this work appeared in preliminary and shortened form at the 1st International Workshop on Neuro-Symbolic Software Engineering (NSE) [Voboril et al., 2025c].

The source code and benchmark instances can be found on Zenodo [Voboril et al., 2025b].

It discusses the use of LLMs for automatic generation of constraints, driving optimization and resource scheduling. anchor_event.full_content:
*   LLM-Based Automatic Constraint Generation is a methodology that leverages large language models to extract and enforce symbolic constraints from diverse data sources.
*   It employs prompt-based extraction, neuro-symbolic pipelines, and multi-agent architectures to translate domain-specific requirements into formal constraints.
*   Empirical results show improved performance in applications like fuzzing, scheduling, and analog circuit design, underscoring its practical impact.

*   **Prompt Templates for Implicit Constraints**: Scheduzz employs prompts requesting the LLM to extract `imply(f₁,f₂)` and `conflict(f₁,f₂)` rules by analyzing raw header text, which are parsed into symbolic facts for reasoning ([Li et al., 24 Jul 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2507.18289)).
*   **Few-shot and In-context Examples**: LLANA uses permutation of few-shot positive and negative analog design parameter configurations to initialize, discriminate, and sample candidate solutions for [Bayesian optimization](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/bayesian-optimization), thus grounding constraints in domain experience ([Chen et al., 2024](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2406.05250)).
*   **Semantic and Syntactic Parsing**: For job scheduling, raw manufacturing procedures are parsed into a dual-view DSL (operation-centric and product-flow-centric), with consequent symbolic verification and push-down automaton-based constraint-checking, effectively eliminating ambiguity ([Shi et al., 3 Oct 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2510.02679)).
*   **Multi-agent and Compositional Extraction**: In NL4OPT (MiniZinc modeling), a suite of constraint-specialized [LLM agents](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/llm-agents) systematically scan natural language for patterns indicative of global constraints, such as all_different, cumulative, or bin_packing, outputting only constraint-specific code fragments for subsequent model integration ([Cai et al., 10 Sep 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2509.08970)).

*   **Prompt and Agent Optimization**: Systematic prompt engineering, few-shot exemplars, and adversarial negative mining to reduce false positives in [agentic approaches](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/agentic-approaches) ([Cai et al., 10 Sep 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2509.08970)).
*   **Symbolic–Neural Co-training**: LLM fine-tuning directly rewards model outputs for formal semantic equivalence, as in solver-guided RL (WARP-1.0-3B) ([Koh et al., 9 Jun 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2506.08171)).
*   **Automated Scenario Adaptation and DSL Discovery**: Scenario-independent DSL induction using DP mixtures and EM maximization permits fast, incremental adaptation to new industrial contexts without handcrafted grammars ([Shi et al., 3 Oct 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2510.02679)).
*   **Auxiliary Filtering for Large-Scale Constraints**: Lightweight, task-specific models (e.g., FoCusNet) are essential for parsing thousands of candidate constraints, suggesting [hybrid architectures](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/hybrid-architectures) for real-world constraint workloads ([Boffa et al., 28 Sep 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2509.24090)).
*   **Multi-objective and Multi-modal Extensions**: A plausible implication is that analog [EDA](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/entropy-controlled-dynamic-allocation-eda) workflows will soon demand multi-objective and multi-modal constraint generation, possibly integrating vector-valued LLM prompting and structural data augmentation.
*   **Integration with Formal Solvers**: There is an increasing tendency to close the generation–verification loop by coupling [LLMs with symbolic solvers](https://www.emergentmind.com/topics/llms-with-symbolic-solvers) (Prolog, SMT, CP), enabling automatic feedback-driven self-correction and model selection ([Shi et al., 7 Oct 2025](https://www.emergentmind.com/papers/2510.05774)).

title: Researchers reveal bias in a widely used measure of algorithm performance
abstract:
Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection

Normalized mutual information is widely used as a similarity measure for evaluating the In this paper, we argue that results performance of clustering and classification algorithms. returned by the normalized mutual information are biased for two reasons: first, because they ignore the information content of the contingency table and, second, because their symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on algorithm output. We introduce a modified version of the mutual information that remedies both of these shortcomings. As a practical demonstration of the importance of using an unbiased measure, we perform extensive numer- ical tests on a basket of popular algorithms for network community detection and show that one’s conclusions about which algorithm is best are significantly affected by the biases in the traditional mutual information.
introduction:
1 Introduction

A common task in data analysis is the comparison of two different labelings of a set of objects. How well do demographics predict political affiliation? How accurately do blood tests predict clinical outcomes? These are questions about how similar one labeling of individuals/items/results is to another and they are commonly tackled using the information theoretic measure known as mutual information, in which an experimental or computational measure of some kind is compared against a ground truth.

Mutual information works by asking of how efficiently we can describe one labeling if we already know the other [1]. Specifically, it measures how much less information it takes to communicate the first labeling if we know the second versus if we do not. As an example, mutual information is commonly used in network science to evaluate the performance of algorithms for network commu- nity detection [2]. One takes a network whose community structure is already known and applies a community detection algorithm to it to infer the communities. Then one uses mutual information to compare the output of the algorithm to the known correct communities. Algorithms that con-

sistently achieve high mutual information scores are considered good. We will use this application as an illustrative example later in the paper.

Mutual information has a number of appealing properties as a tool for comparing labelings. It is invariant under permutations of the labels, so that, for example, two divisions of a network into communities that differ only in the numbering of the communities will be correctly identified as being the same. It also returns sensible results in cases where the number of distinct label values is not the same in the two labelings. However, the standard mutual information measure also has some significant shortcomings, and two in particular that we highlight in this paper. First, it has a bias towards labelings with too many distinct label values. For instance, a community detection algorithm that incorrectly divides networks into significantly more groups than are present in the ground truth can nonetheless achieve a high mutual information score. A number of approaches for correcting this flaw have been proposed. One can apply direct penalties for incorrect numbers of groups [3] or subtract correction terms based on the average mutual information over some ensemble of candidate labelings [4, 5] or on the statistics of the contingency table [6, 7]. For reasons we discuss shortly, we favor the latter approach, which leads to the measure known as the reduced mutual information.

The second drawback of the mutual information arises when the measure is normalized, as it commonly is to improve interpretability. The most popular normalization scheme creates a measure that runs between zero and one by dividing the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings being compared [8], although one can also normalize by the minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the entropies. As we demonstrate in this paper, however, these normalizations introduce biases into the results by comparison with the unnormalized measure, because the normalization factor depends on the candidate labeling as well as the ground truth. This effect can be large enough to change scientific outcomes, and we provide examples of this phenomenon.

In order to avoid this latter bias, while still retaining the interpretability of a normalized mutual information measure, we favor normalizing by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This removes the source of bias but introduces an asymmetry in the normalization. At first sight this asymmetry may seem undesirable, and previous authors have gone to some lengths to avoid it. Here, however, we argue that it is not only justified but actually desirable, for several reasons. First, many of the classification problems we consider are unaffected by the asymmetry, since they involve the comparison of one or more candidate labelings against a single, unchanging ground truth. Moreover, by contrast with the multitude of possible symmetric normalizations, the asymmetric measure we propose is the unique way to normalize the mutual information without introducing biases due to the normalization itself.

More broadly, the asymmetric measure is more informative than the conventional symmetric one. Consider for instance the common situation where the groups or communities in one labeling are a subdivision of those in the other. We might for example label individuals by the country they live in on the one hand and by the town or city on the other. Then the more detailed labeling tells us everything there is to know about the coarser one, but the reverse is not true. Telling you the city fixes the country, but not vice versa. Thus one could argue that the mutual information between the two eshould be asymmetric, and this type of asymmetry will be a key feature of the measures we study.

Both drawbacks of the standard mutual information—bias towards too many groups and depen- dence of the normalization on the candidate labeling—can be addressed simultaneously by using an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information as defined in this paper. In support of this approach we present an extensive comparison of the performance of this and other variants of the mutual information in network community detection tasks, generating a large number of random

test networks with known community structure and a variety of structural parameters, and then attempting to recover the communities using popular community detection algorithms. Within this framework, we find that conclusions about which algorithms perform best are significantly impacted by the choice of mutual information measure, and specifically that traditional measures erroneously favor algorithms that find too many communities, but our proposed measure does not. Code im- plementing our measure can be found at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Widespread Reliance on Normalized Mutual Information Under Scrutiny as Research Reveals Systematic Bias

News Article:  

For decades, scientists across domains as diverse as network science, bioinformatics, and machine learning have leveraged Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) as a trusted statistical tool for evaluating the output of classification and clustering algorithms. Yet, new research published in *Nature Communications* ([Jerdee, Kirkley, Newman, 2024](https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)) fundamentally challenges this consensus, spotlighting significant and previously overlooked sources of bias in NMI. In practical terms, results derived from NMI may not be as reliable as commonly presumed—a conclusion with substantial implications for the trustworthiness of empirical and benchmark studies in data science that depend on algorithm comparisons. As summarized succinctly in recent coverage, these revelations are not marginal: analysis shows that NMI can introduce errors large enough to alter which algorithms are judged as superior, potentially subverting years of comparative progress ([Real-World Anchor Event](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/william-bill-kemp-75b66a6_researchers-reveal-bias-in-a-widely-used-activity-7406708443543322624-eW_P)).

The core of the issue highlighted in the new work is the systematic bias embedded in the normalized formulation of mutual information. While mutual information itself quantifies how efficiently one labeling can be described given knowledge of another, its normalized variant is designed to provide bounded, more interpretable comparisons between labelings—crucial for applications from community detection in networks to medical outcome predictions. However, the study demonstrates through both theoretical and computational analysis that the widespread normalizations, particularly those based on the arithmetic mean or other symmetric functions of the entropies of the labelings, inadvertently introduce a dependency on the candidate algorithm output. 

The problem is twofold. First, traditional mutual information is inherently biased towards labelings that have a larger number of distinct groups, a point previously noted in the literature and directly confronted in the current research [Abstract; Introduction]. For example, a community detection algorithm that fragmentarily partitions a network into an excessive number of communities—beyond the underlying ground truth—can still secure an inflated NMI score. This inflation is not merely theoretical; the authors document it across a battery of popular community detection algorithms and synthetic benchmark networks. The consequence is that algorithms capable of overpartitioning or collapsing communities can be unjustifiably favored when NMI is used as the evaluation metric. The design of these clustering and community detection benchmarks thus needs re-examination, as algorithmic assessments built on NMI may be misleading.

Second, and perhaps more critically, the standard normalization methods themselves—intended to ensure comparability and interpretability—are the sources of further bias. As detailed in the research, the most common approach divides the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings. This “symmetric normalization” introduces a spurious dependence on the candidate algorithm’s output, making the evaluation metric contingent not only on the ground truth but on the characteristics of each algorithm’s partitioning behavior. The magnitude of this bias is nontrivial; in the empirical demonstrations provided, the favored algorithms according to NMI can change entirely under different normalizations [Abstract; Introduction]. This effect is sufficient to invert conclusions about algorithmic superiority, a striking finding that calls into question a substantial fraction of comparative studies predicated on NMI-based benchmarks.

To remedy these overlapping sources of distortion, the authors propose a principled correction: an “asymmetric” normalized reduced mutual information. Rather than normalizing by any symmetric function of both candidate and ground truth, this measure is normalized uniquely and solely by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling. While this approach forgoes the formal symmetry of traditional NMI, the paper argues—and demonstrates—that this asymmetry is both mathematically justified and practically preferable. In many real-world classification tasks, asymmetry mirrors the nature of evaluation: candidate labelings are always compared to a fixed ground truth, not to each other. By grounding the normalization exclusively in the properties of the ground truth, the new measure eliminates the dependence on algorithm-specific outputs and the associated spurious bias.

The empirical impact of adopting the asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information is direct and robust. When applied to standard benchmarks in network community detection, this correction reverses prior verdicts of algorithmic supremacy established by traditional NMI assessments. Specifically, algorithms previously deemed best by NMI’s flawed standard are revealed to be systematically privileged by the metric’s bias towards overpartitioning, while the corrected measure yields a more reliable reflection of true performance [Abstract; Introduction]. The implications ripple outwards: evaluation pipelines and published results throughout scientific fields reliant on clustering and classification must re-examine their methodological assumptions and potentially recalibrate their findings.

In sum, this research punctures a long-standing assumption in computational evaluation metrics and provides a technically robust correction. For researchers and practitioners seeking rigorous assessment of clustering and classification algorithms, the adoption of this unbiased, asymmetric measure should now be viewed as a methodological imperative. The full details of the new metric, including implementation code, are accessible through the authors’ [repository](https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi).

Given the potential impact of these findings, readers are encouraged to delve directly into the original research [here](https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8) to understand the broader methodological shift now recommended for the data science and network analysis communities.

anchor_event.summary: Recent findings highlight significant bias in Normalized Mutual Information (NMI), a widely used metric for evaluating algorithm performance. As noted in [2] and further supported by [3], as the number of communities escalates, NMI can become increasingly biased—either favoring inflated group counts or skewing algorithm assessment—which is in line with the concerns and solutions discussed in the primary research.
anchor_event.full_content:
Recent findings highlight significant bias in Normalized Mutual Information (NMI), a widely used metric for evaluating algorithm performance in data classification and clustering. The research demonstrates that NMI can favor algorithms that over-divide data or oversimplify categories, potentially leading to misleading conclusions. To address these issues, a revised, asymmetric version of the metric has been proposed, offering more reliable and consistent comparisons. This advancement aims to enhance the accuracy of algorithm assessment across various fields where clustering and classification are essential.

"When scientists test algorithms that sort or classify data, they often turn to a trusted tool called Normalized Mutual Information (or NMI) to measure how well an algorithm's output matches reality. But according to new research, that tool may not be as reliable as many assume. In a paper published in Nature Communications,SFI Postdoctoral Fellow Max Jerdee, Alec Kirkley (University of Hong Kong), and SFI External Professor Mark Newman (University of Michigan) show that NMI—one of the most widely used metrics in data science and network research—can produce biased results. "Normalized mutual information has been used or referenced in thousands of papers in the decades since it was first proposed," Newman says, "but it turns out that it can give incorrect results, and the errors are large enough to change scientific conclusions in some cases."" [#bias](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fbias&trk=public_post-text)

title: New system efficiently explains AI judgments in realtime
abstract: Model-agnostic explanation methods are essential for interpreting machine learning models, but suffer from prohibitive computational costs that scale with the number of baselines. Existing acceleration approaches either lack a theoretical base or provide no principled guidance for baseline selection. To address this gap, we present ABSQR (Amortized Baseline Selection via Rank-Revealing QR). This framework exploits the low-rank structure of value matrices to accelerate multi-baseline attribution methods. Our approach combines deterministic baseline selection via SVD-guided QR decomposition with an amortized inference mechanism that utilizes cluster-based retrieval. We reduce computational complexity from O (m • 2d) to O (k • 2d), where k ≪ m. Experiments demonstrate that ABSQR achieves a 91.2% agreement rate with full baseline methods while providing 8.5× speedup across diverse datasets. As the first acceleration approach that preserves explanation error guarantees under computational speedup, ABSQR makes the practical deployment of interpretable AI systems feasible at scale.
introduction: The deployment of AI in sensitive domains like medical diagnostics and finance has created urgent needs for interpretable decision-making and explainable AI (XAI). One classic XAI method is feature attribution, which quantifies the contribution of each input to a prediction, enabling model debugging, fairness assessment, and regulatory compliance. In particular, model-agnostic approaches have gained prominence due to their universal applicability across different architectures, providing consistent interpretations across diverse models. Among model-agnostic attribution methods, KernelSHAP and LIME estimate importance by generating local perturbations, evaluating outputs, and performing a weighted regression on the resulting data. For tabular data, using multiple baselines can capture diverse decision boundaries and provide robust explanations, but at high computational costs. The bottleneck lies in constructing the value matrix, which requires separate model evaluations for each perturbation-baseline pair. With hundreds of baselines and thousands of feature combinations, this burden can make real-time applications infeasible. Despite extensive research into acceleration techniques, existing strategies face fundamental limitations. For instance, FastSHAP generates explanations directly from data using learned models but lacks theoretical grounding, while random sampling approaches increase variance and cause inconsistent explanations. Moreover, none of the methods provide principled guidance for baseline selection. To address these challenges, we propose Amortized Baseline Selection via Rank-Revealing QR (ABSQR), a framework that accelerates multi-baseline attribution methods by exploiting the low-rank structure of the value matrix. ABSQR operates in two stages: (1) framing baseline selection as a Column Subset Selection Problem (CSSP) solved via SVD-guided Rank-Revealing QR decomposition, and (2) introducing amortized inference through precomputed coefficients for test-time retrieval via cluster-based matching. Experiments demonstrate 91.2% fidelity with an 8.5× speedup across datasets. The contributions of this work are: (1) a general acceleration framework for multi-baseline attribution methods in XAI deployment; (2) a theoretical, deterministic baseline selection method establishing the first principled acceleration setting; (3) an amortized inference strategy that bypasses test-time model evaluation; and (4) empirical validation showing 91.2% fidelity with an 8.5× speedup across datasets.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
ABSQR Sets a New Paradigm for Scalable, Responsible AI: The First Theoretically Grounded Method for Fast, Model-Agnostic Explanations

News Article:  
ABSQR (Amortized Baseline Selection via Rank-Revealing QR) establishes a new technical benchmark as the first theoretically grounded and scalable method for accelerating model-agnostic AI explanations. This advance arrives at a pivotal juncture, directly addressing the urgent need for interpretable and rapid AI explanations that real-time industry applications—ranging from healthcare to diagnostics and autonomous systems—now demand. As AI continues its maturation, real-world deployments increasingly encounter the dual imperative of speed and transparency, a challenge also highlighted in MIT’s recent explainable AI research using Concept Bottleneck Models. Both innovations embody the evolution of AI from black-box predictors to actionable, trustworthy systems, emphasizing the societal significance of explainability in critical decision contexts [Real-World Anchor Event].

The central issue underlying deployment of AI in sensitive domains is not solely predictive performance, but the ability to provide interpretable, accountable, and transparent outputs at scale [Introduction]. Model-agnostic explanation methods, particularly multi-baseline feature attribution algorithms like KernelSHAP and LIME, have proven essential for this purpose. These methods assess the contribution of each input feature via local perturbations and weighted regression, supporting model debugging, fairness auditing, and regulatory compliance. However, the computational cost for constructing the value matrix—the core step where model outputs are computed for all perturbation-baseline combinations—is a substantial barrier, especially as the number of baselines grows. This constraint is acute in domains such as medical diagnostics, autonomous vehicles, and algorithmic recommendations, where decision latency and auditability are paramount.

Prevailing acceleration approaches present significant limitations. FastSHAP, for example, sidesteps baseline computation by learning explanations directly from training data, lacking any theoretical underpinnings regarding explanation fidelity. Sampling-based approaches suffer from high variance and inconsistent attributions, compromising fairness and raising concerns for real-world deployments. Critically, no existing method prior to ABSQR provides a principled or theoretically guaranteed mechanism for baseline selection—leaving a conspicuous gap at the intersection of computational efficiency, interpretability, and accountability [Abstract, Introduction].

ABSQR advances the state of the art by leveraging the low-rank structure of attribution value matrices to enable both speed and fidelity without sacrificing theoretical rigor. The framework operates in two conceptual stages. First, baseline selection is recast as a Column Subset Selection Problem (CSSP), solved through SVD-guided Rank-Revealing QR decomposition. This ensures deterministic, theory-backed selection of baseline instances—a critical contribution for establishing reproducibility and consistency in high-stakes interpretability workflows. In the second stage, ABSQR realizes amortized inference: precomputed attribution coefficients are indexed via cluster-based retrieval, enabling fast test-time explanations that bypass repeated model evaluation [Abstract, Introduction].

This pipeline reduces computational complexity from O(m · 2^d) to O(k · 2^d), where k is much less than m, making explanation costs tractable for industrial-scale deployments. Empirical results demonstrate a 91.2% agreement rate (fidelity) with standard multi-baseline attribution, while achieving an 8.5× speedup across diverse datasets [Abstract, Introduction]. Unlike prior methods, ABSQR is the first to establish explanation error guarantees under computational acceleration, directly addressing the industry’s demand for trust, auditability, and transparency mandated by evolving regulatory landscapes and ethical frameworks.

The ramifications are significant. For sectors such as healthcare, financial risk assessment, autonomous vehicles, and algorithmic recommendations—where model decisions must not only be rapid but also interpretable and defensible—ABSQR presents a scalable and theoretically principled pathway. By providing deterministic and guaranteed explanation quality at reduced computational cost, ABSQR directly supports responsible AI deployment, advancing the concrete requirements of fairness, accountability, and transparency [Abstract, Introduction, Anchor Event].

As AI becomes deeply integrated into social-critical infrastructures, the technical community must prioritize methods that do not trade off interpretability for efficiency. ABSQR marks a watershed moment, offering an architecture-agnostic solution that enables model transparency at the scale and pace needed for modern AI applications. This is in direct alignment with the sector-wide call—from research, industry, and regulatory bodies—for trustworthy, auditable systems that uphold societal and ethical obligations [Real-World Anchor Event].

For researchers, practitioners, and policymakers seeking to deploy robust, interpretable AI at scale, ABSQR presents a rigorously validated technical solution. Its introduction signals a step change in the practical realization of explainable and responsible AI. Access the full research at [ACM Digital Library](https://dx.doi.org/10.1145/3746252.3761036) to evaluate and operationalize these advances as the community continues to shape the next stages of AI deployment.

[#snsinstitutions](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsnsinstitutions&trk=public_post-text)[#snsdesignthinkers](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsnsdesignthinkers&trk=public_post-text)[#designthinking](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdesignthinking&trk=public_post-text)[#designthinkers](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fdesignthinkers&trk=public_post-text)[#snsdesignthing](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fsnsdesignthing&trk=public_post-text) The maturation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) represents the progressive development of intelligent systems from basic rule-based programs to highly adaptive and learning-driven technologies. Initially, AI systems were designed to follow fixed instructions and could only perform specific tasks defined by programmers. The conceptual groundwork was influenced by pioneers such as Alan Turing, who proposed that machines could simulate human thinking processes. However, early systems lacked flexibility and the ability to learn from experience. As computing power increased and data became widely available, AI entered a new phase driven by machine learning. Instead of relying solely on predefined rules, systems began to learn patterns from data and improve their performance over time. The emergence of deep learning further enhanced AI capabilities by using artificial neural networks to process complex information such as images, speech, and natural language. This advancement significantly improved accuracy and efficiency in various applications. Organizations like OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and IBM have played a major role in accelerating AI research and innovation. AI is now widely used in healthcare diagnostics, autonomous vehicles, financial analysis, robotics, and intelligent virtual assistants. Its integration into daily life demonstrates how far the technology has matured from its experimental beginnings. Another important dimension of AI maturation is the development of ethical guidelines and regulatory frameworks. As AI systems influence critical decisions, concerns about bias, privacy, and accountability have gained importance. Governments and institutions are working toward responsible AI deployment to ensure fairness and transparency in its applications. In conclusion, the maturation of Artificial Intelligence reflects a journey of technological evolution, practical adoption, and ethical awareness. From simple programmed machines to advanced learning systems, AI continues to evolve and reshape industries. With responsible development and continuous innovation, AI is expected to further enhance human capabilities and contribute significantly to the future of society.

🚨 Are we approaching the limits of Large Language Models? Over the last few years, tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude have reshaped how we work with information. From writing and coding to research and decision-making, generative AI has quickly become part of everyday workflows. But many AI researchers believe we may be reaching the limitations of language-only AI systems. Most Large Language Models (LLMs) are incredibly good at predicting the next word in a sequence. They are powerful pattern recognition systems. However, they still lack something fundamental: ➡️ An understanding of the real world. ➡️ Common-sense reasoning. ➡️ The ability to plan and interact with physical environments. This is the challenge that AI pioneer Yann LeCun is trying to solve. LeCun, the Chief AI Scientist at Meta and one of the pioneers of modern deep learning, is involved in a new venture called AMI (Advanced Machine Intelligence). The startup has reportedly raised around $1 billion in funding, backed by major players including: • Jeff Bezos • Nvidia • Toyota • Samsung But the goal is not to build another chatbot. Instead, the focus is on developing “World Models.” These are AI systems designed to understand how the physical world works, allowing machines to reason about environments, predict outcomes, and plan actions. Imagine telling a robot: “Make me a cup of tea.” Today’s AI might give you instructions. But a world-model AI system could potentially: • Understand the environment • Identify objects and tools • Plan the required steps • Execute the task in the real world This shift points toward a new frontier in AI: 🔹 Physical AI 🔹 Spatial Intelligence 🔹 World-Understanding Systems If successful, this approach could redefine robotics, autonomous systems, and how humans collaborate with machines. We may be entering the next phase of AI — not just machines that generate text, but machines that understand reality. 💬 What do you think? Will LLMs continue to dominate the AI landscape, or will world-model-based AI become the next big breakthrough?
