The draft effectively introduces ABSQR as a breakthrough in scalable, model-agnostic AI explanations and ties it to the urgent need for faster, interpretable AI. However, it does not fully satisfy the BRIDGE CONSTRAINT. While the draft mentions the importance of fairness, accountability, and transparency, it does not explicitly connect ABSQR's methodology to the necessity for responsible AI deployment in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, autonomous vehicles, diagnostics, and recommendation systems. Additionally, the draft could more sharply emphasize how ABSQR meets industry demands for interpretable, trustworthy systems as articulated in the maturation of AI and related resources.

The draft partially satisfies the target rubrics but falls short in explicitly and cohesively bridging ABSQR's innovation to the urgent need for faster, more interpretable AI, particularly in high-stakes domains. While it mentions healthcare, autonomous vehicles, and other sectors, the connection between ABSQR's scalable method and the necessity for responsible AI deployment, fairness, and accountability is not consistently emphasized throughout the article. Additionally, the draft could more directly and strongly tie the breakthrough to the maturation of AI and real-time industry demands for transparency and speed.

The draft partially satisfies the HOOK CONSTRAINT by positioning ABSQR as a breakthrough for accelerating model-agnostic AI explanations, but it does not explicitly frame it as the first theoretically grounded and scalable method, nor does it sufficiently emphasize the urgency of faster, more interpretable AI in real-time industry applications. The BRIDGE CONSTRAINT is partially addressed by discussing the importance of explainability and connecting ABSQR to high-stakes domains, but the language lacks explicit emphasis on fairness, accountability, and transparency as critical pillars of responsible AI deployment. Additionally, the connection to industry demand for interpretable, trustworthy systems could be strengthened and more consistently woven throughout the article.

The draft meets some of the target rubrics but falls short in fully satisfying the HOOK and BRIDGE constraints. While it highlights ABSQR as a groundbreaking method, the opening does not explicitly and sufficiently emphasize ABSQR as the first theoretically grounded and scalable method for accelerating model-agnostic AI explanations, nor does it strongly connect this advance to the urgent need for faster, more interpretable AI in real-time industry applications. Additionally, while the draft touches on the importance of responsible AI deployment, it does not consistently and explicitly bridge ABSQR's innovation to the necessity for fairness, accountability, and transparency, particularly in high-stakes domains like healthcare and autonomous vehicles. The draft also lacks consistent emphasis on the practical relevance and urgency of ABSQR's breakthrough throughout the article.

title: New method improves the reliability of statistical estimations
abstract: Estimating associations between spatial covariates and responses — rather than merely predicting responses — is central to environmental science, epidemiology, and economics. For instance, public health officials might be interested in whether air pollution has a strictly positive association with a health outcome, and the mag- nitude of any effect. Standard machine learning methods often provide accurate predictions but offer limited insight into covariate-response relationships. And we show that existing methods for constructing confidence (or credible) intervals for associations can fail to provide nominal coverage in the face of model misspecifi- cation and nonrandom locations — despite both being essentially always present in spatial problems. We introduce a method that constructs valid frequentist confi- dence intervals for associations in spatial settings. Our method requires minimal assumptions beyond a form of spatial smoothness and a homoskedastic Gaussian error assumption. In particular, we do not require model correctness or covariate overlap between training and target locations. Our approach is the first to guarantee nominal coverage in this setting and outperforms existing techniques in both real and simulated experiments. Our confidence intervals are valid in finite samples when the noise of the Gaussian error is known, and we provide an asymptotically consistent estimation procedure for this noise variance when it is unknown.
introduction:
Introduction

Scientists and social scientists often seek to understand the direction and magnitude of associations in settings where variables vary spatially. And since these associations are often used to inform policy, communicating uncertainties is crucial. Example associations of interest include the relationship between aerosol concentrations and regional precipitation changes [76], the link between proximity to major highways and the prevalence of dementia [35], and the link between air pollution exposure and birth weight [34]. In each case, the covariates (e.g., aerosol concentrations, proximity to highways, and air pollution) may be viewed as functions of spatial location. Moreover, scientists often have data at some spatial locations but want to infer associations at others. For instance, a country might measure birth weight and air pollution at the municipal level for some municipalities and wish to understand their relationship in municipalities without data.

Our goal in the present work is to provide valid and useful confidence intervals for an estimator of these associations when (i) the underlying model may be misspecified and (ii) inference is needed at spatial locations that may differ from those in the observed data. First we argue that existing methods do not already solve this problem. In particular, we make this argument in turn for modern flexible machine learning methods, linear methods, spatial regression methods, and debiasing approaches.

∗Equal Contribution

Flexible Machine Learning Methods. A priori, we might expect a nonlinear relationship between air pollution (as a covariate) and birth weight (as the response). A natural idea is to fit a flexible model — such as a (deep) Gaussian process [52, 15], transformer [70, 33], or XGBoost [10]. These methods can achieve high predictive accuracy, but the methods on their own often lack interpretability and do not immediately yield conclusions about covariate-response associations [57, 17]. Researchers have proposed a number of post hoc interpretability methods, such as Shapley values [61, 38], LIME [53], partial dependence plots [20] and Accumulated Local Effects plots [3]. These methods work directly with the fitted model and can be used to describe associations between individual covariates and model predictions. And because they are interpreting the fitted model (rather than the response itself), there is no need (or mechanism) to quantify uncertainties due to insufficient data. When the fitted model closely approximates the true response, one can view the output of these interpretability methods as describing the relationship between the covariates and response.

In many applications (e.g., in engineering, advertising, and marketing), the available data often contains enough information to be confident that the highly flexible machine learning model ap- proximates the response everywhere of interest. Conversely, in many applications in the sciences and social sciences (such as those cited above), there is often insufficient data to confidently fit a nonparametric or high-dimensional model closely to the true (latent) response everywhere relevant. In particular, in many spatial problems, there is often not enough information for a flexible method to reconstruct the response well in many spatial locations of interest. Nonetheless, we might think there could still be sufficient data to capture associations — and, by quantifying uncertainty, we can check our confidence in any conclusions. The discrepancy between applications that are data-rich and data-poor in this sense can help explain the phenomenon observed by Rudin [57]: that post hoc approximations of black-box models may be worse than fitting interpretable models directly. For example, as illustrated in a real-life recidivism case, a linear model used to approximate a black-box model can seriously misrepresent key relationships — including that between race and recidivism.

Ordinary Least Squares. So it seems natural to choose an appropriate interpretable model (rather than post hoc method) and quantify its uncertainty. Buja et al. [7] demonstrated that (directly-fit, not post hoc) linear models can be used to interpretably summarize associations even in the face of potentially nonlinear relationships (i.e., under misspecification). This observation helps explain why all of the applied studies cited above use linear regression.2 Moreover, ordinary least squares (OLS) comes equipped with classical confidence intervals. However, when the linear model is misspecified, Buja et al. [7] notes that the OLS estimator depends on the covariate values, and it follows that classical confidence intervals are valid only at the observed (training) values. Since we’re interested in valid intervals at other locations, which will have different covariate values, we need another approach. The sandwich estimator [30, 77, 78, 7] offers valid intervals under misspecification, but only if all covariates are drawn from a single distribution, which isn’t the case in our setting where we want to draw inferences at different locations from where we observe data. Thus, even though OLS provides interpretable estimates, it does not solve our key challenge: constructing valid confidence intervals in the common scenario where both (i) the linear model is misspecified and (ii) inference is needed at spatial locations that may differ from those in the observed data.

Spatial Regression Methods. One might hope that a method specifically tailored for spatial regression could address these issues. Generalized least squares (GLS) regression [2] is designed to handle spatial correlation in residuals [13, pp. 22–24], but it does not address the bias introduced by misspecification and nonrandom locations. Bayesian spatial models, such as those based on Gaussian processes, are also common, but their credible intervals tend to underestimate uncertainty in the presence of both misspecification and nonrandom locations [74, 42].

Debiasing Approaches. Another natural idea is to construct a debiased estimator of the association, and then account for the variance of this debiasing procedure. Importance weighting methods from the covariate shift literature [62] pursue this goal by reweighting each observation according to an estimated density ratio between target and source covariate distributions, aiming to remove bias from distribution shift. Semiparametric inference in partially linear models (e.g., [55, 54, 11]) takes a related approach: it first fits a flexible model to capture variation explained by a “nuisance” parameter (in our case, the spatial location) and then constructs a debiased estimator of the target association by regressing out the variation explained by this nuisance, so that the remaining signal reflects the

2In fact, Castro Torres and Akbaritabar [9, Figure 1] surveyed quantitative methods in papers up to 2022 and found that over half of those in fields such as agricultural science, social sciences, and health sciences reported results from a linear model.

direct relationship between the covariate and the response. However, these methods face two key limitations. First, they assume that the debiasing step can perfectly remove bias, which need not be true (especially in finite samples). Second, their validity relies on the assumption that observations are drawn independently and identically. And this assumption rarely holds in spatial applications, where locations are fixed and often spread unevenly due to physical, logistical, or policy constraints. We discuss these and related approaches in more detail in Appendix A.

Our Contribution. In what follows, we show through real and simulated experiments (Section 4) that existing approaches can yield confidence intervals with coverage far below the nominal level. Our principal contribution is to introduce the first method for constructing confidence intervals in spatial associations that guarantees frequentist coverage at the nominal level even when the underlying model is misspecified and inference is required at fixed, nonrandom locations that differ from those observed. We are able to account for misspecification and nonrandom locations simultaneously by making more spatially appropriate assumptions than prior work. In particular, prior work relies on a (spatially inappropriate) assumption of independent and identically distribution data; we instead assume the response is a smooth function of space observed with homoskedastic Gaussian noise. When the variance of this noise is known, our confidence intervals are valid in finite samples. To address the common case where the variance is unknown, we provide an asymptotically consistent estimator for it. In our experiments, our method is the only one that consistently attains nominal coverage (or even comes close).
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
MIT Method Exposes Flaws in Spatial Confidence Intervals, Establishes First Guarantee for Valid Inference at Nonrandom Locations

News Article:  
The integrity of scientific inference in spatial analyses has been compromised by a critical flaw: conventional statistical methods routinely produce confidence intervals that are “completely wrong” when applied to real-world spatial data. As highlighted in the recent report “False Precision in Science: MIT Exposes the Spatial Statistics Flaw,” these errors arise because standard approaches claim nominal coverage rates but fail dramatically under the realistic conditions of spatial correlation, model misspecification, and nonrandomly selected locations. Researchers have demonstrated that methods promising, for example, 95% coverage can, in practice, provide zero percent—yielding false precision and misleading certainty. Addressing this urgent gap, a research team from MIT has introduced the first statistical method that guarantees valid frequentist confidence intervals for associations in spatial settings, regardless of model misspecification and at unobserved, nonrandom locations ([Citation](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.06067)).

The implications of ignoring spatial correlation extend beyond mere methodological nuance; in environmental science, epidemiology, and economics, misattribution of associations due to spatial dependencies can fundamentally distort understanding of causality and intervention outcomes. For instance, economic models that disregard geographic dependence risk attributing growth or inequality to spurious factors, as contiguous regions often share underlying economic characteristics. The result, as documented in recent spatial economic research, is “serious misspecification problems and inappropriate interpretation”—where effects are assigned to the wrong causes and estimated relationships appear with unwarranted certainty ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

Standard machine learning techniques have made significant advances in predictive accuracy but offer limited interpretability about covariate-response associations, and their post hoc interpretability tools lack mechanisms for quantifying uncertainty arising from insufficient data or from extrapolating to new spatial locations ([Abstract], [Introduction]). In spatial problems, where data is frequently sparse or unevenly distributed, flexible models such as deep Gaussian processes, transformers, or XGBoost cannot reconstruct the response surface accurately enough at unobserved sites. Even linear ordinary least squares (OLS) methods, while interpretable, only provide valid confidence intervals at the observed data points, and their classical coverage guarantees break down at new, target locations or under model misspecification ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

Other conventional remedies—including generalized least squares regression tailored for spatial correlation and Bayesian credible intervals—are insufficient. Generalized least squares does not address biases from model misspecification or nonrandom locations. Bayesian spatial models, frequently based on Gaussian processes, systematically underestimate uncertainty when confronted with combined model misspecification and nonrandom sampling. Similarly, approaches that “debias” estimates through importance weighting or semiparametric inference require strong, often violated assumptions—specifically, the ability to fully correct for bias and the necessity that data be independently and identically distributed, assumptions rarely met in spatial studies ([Introduction]).

The MIT method overcomes these shortcomings by explicitly constructing valid frequentist confidence intervals for estimators of spatial associations, even when the underlying model is misspecified and inference is required at fixed, nonrandom locations that differ from those observed in the data ([Abstract]). The methodological advance is rooted in replacing the spatially inappropriate assumptions of independent and identically distributed samples that underpin prior methods with the assumption that the response function is spatially smooth and observed with homoskedastic Gaussian noise. Crucially, this approach does not require model correctness or covariate overlap between training and target locations.

Empirically, the MIT method reliably produces confidence intervals that maintain nominal coverage rates—delivering the level of certainty claimed by the statistic—irrespective of the noise structure, provided that noise variance is known ([Abstract]). For the more common scenario of unknown noise variance, the research provides an asymptotically consistent estimation procedure. Across both real and simulated experiments, the MIT approach is the only method that consistently attains or closely approaches the intended coverage, eliminating the false precision endemic to earlier methods ([Abstract]).

This methodological leap not only safeguards against the misattribution of economic relationships due to spatial dependencies but directly addresses the profound impact of statistical mis-specification in modeling development, inequality, and health outcomes. By delivering confidence intervals that remain valid under the spatial dependencies and misspecification omnipresent in real data, this approach reduces barriers to credible inference and narrows the persistent gap between claimed certainty and actual certainty identified as a central obstacle in the reproducibility crisis ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

Given the ubiquity of spatial questions in critical policy domains—from air pollution and public health to economic development—access to statistically valid, interpretable, and robust confidence intervals is essential. Researchers, policymakers, and practitioners are urged to examine the MIT method in detail and apply these advances to produce inferences that reliably inform high-stakes decisions. The full research paper is available for rigorous scrutiny and adoption ([Citation](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.06067)).

anchor_event.summary: Result [1] ('False Precision in Science: MIT Exposes the Spatial Statistics Flaw') provides a high-quality, newsworthy hook revealing that prior methods for spatial confidence interval estimation have fundamental flaws—creating a compelling rationale for the necessity of the new method discussed in the primary research. This source offers strong technical depth by directly addressing the limitations and reputational risks associated with flawed confidence interval methods in spatial sciences, and it comes from a credible institutional source (MIT). Such a revelation about the limitations in existing science can be used to build urgency for advances like those in the primary research.
anchor_event.full_content:
The MIT method addresses one specific source of such failures. By providing confidence intervals that remain valid under conditions that actually occur in spatial analyses, rather than idealised conditions that rarely exist, the approach reduces the gap between claimed and actual certainty. This is not a complete solution to the reproducibility crisis, but it removes one barrier to credible inference.

But as research published in Spatial Economic Analysis notes, ignoring spatial correlation can lead to “serious misspecification problems and inappropriate interpretation.” Models that fail to account for geographic dependencies may attribute effects to the wrong causes or estimate relationships with false precision. The finding that neighbouring regions tend to share economic characteristics, with high-growth areas clustered near other high-growth areas and low-growth areas similarly clustered, has profound implications for how economists model development and inequality.

When data varies across space, conventional statistical methods produce confidence intervals that can be, in the researchers' words, “completely wrong.” Methods that claim 95 per cent coverage achieve zero per cent. Methods designed for independent data are applied to dependent data, producing precise-looking numbers that mean nothing. The new approach produces intervals that remain valid under realistic conditions, intervals that actually deliver the coverage they promise.

title: AI can pick up cultural values by mimicking how kids learn
abstract:
Constructing a universal moral code for artificial intelligence (AI) is challenging because human cultures have different values, norms, and social practices. We

therefore argue that AI systems should adapt to culture based on observation: Just

as a child raised in a particular culture learns the specific values, norms, and behav-
introduction:
1 Introduction

A formidable challenge in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) is endowing AI agents with the richness and depth of values and behaviors that characterize human cultures and their decision-making tendencies. State-of-the-art AI systems today [1–3] are based on large-language models (LLMs), which are neural networks with billions of parameters trained on data harvested from across the internet, leading

Data availability statement: Code and data for replicating the findings in this article are available at: https:// github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl.

to a “one-size-fits-all” (rather than pluralistic) AI system [4,5] (see also [6,7]). In this article, we propose that rather than attempting to handcraft or learn a universal code of values and behaviors for AI, AI agents should implicitly learn them by being embedded in the human culture in which they are operating [8]. We use the term “culture” here to refer to the dynamic values, norms, and behaviors shared by a social group [9], acknowledging that it is a dynamic phenomenon—a cultural cycle in which humans continuously shape and adopt values and behaviors [10,11]. The approach we propose would endow AI agents with the ability to deal with such cul- tural dynamics in a manner that is tailored to the culture or social group in which the AI agent was “raised.” This recognizes the fact that what is acceptable or norma- tive in one culture may not be acceptable in another [12], necessitating AI systems that are capable of adapting to the values and behavioral tendencies of the culture in which they have been deployed. This will be challenging for AI and some intellec- tual humility is demanded, in as much as even humans, the creators of the AI, have a hard time appreciating and accepting the values and behaviors of cultures different from their own [12,13]. Might future AI surpass humans in such flexibility?

Funding: This work was supported by the Templeton World Charity Foundation (RPNR, ANM), the National Science Foundation EFRI Grant no. 2223495 (RPNR) and Grant no. 2230466 (KR, ANM), a UW + Amazon Science Hub grant (RPNR), a grant from the Bezos Family Foundation (ANM), and the Cherng Jia & Elizabeth Yun Hwang Professorship (RPNR). https://www.templetonworldcharity.org/ https://www.nsf.gov/ https://www.sciencehub.uw.edu/ https://www.bezosfamilyfoundation.org/ The funders did not play any role in the study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

Competing interests: The authors have declared that no competing interests exist.

1.1 Altruism as a cultural value for AI to learn

In this article, we focus on the human tendency to act in a way that benefits others, even if it comes at a cost to oneself, often labeled “altruistic behavior.” For example, a person might give precious resources (such as food, money, or time) to someone else without directly benefiting themselves. In cases of extreme altruism, these acts can be directed towards strangers even in situations when the actor has no expectancy for reciprocal action or reward. There are significant between-country differences in altruistic tendencies across industrialized nations [14], small-scale traditional cultures [15], and even in children as a function of cultural background [16], motivating the need to investigate whether an AI system could learn altruistic behaviors from observation.

Our approach draws on evidence about how neural mechanisms in the brain support normative social behavior: the same reward systems thought to be involved in reinforcement-based learning are also harnessed to learn the dominant norms and values within a society [17–19]. The resulting system governs human cultural expectations and ways of interacting with others [20,21]. To learn what the accepted behavior is in any given context, the brain must first develop expectation rules about events and learn internal “value systems,” including the benefits of certain behav- iors as well as the costs of deviating from the norm. For example, by perceiving or producing altruistic behaviors within a culture—such as giving others items of value or otherwise acting to benefit others even at a cost to oneself—a child or an ado- lescent may learn to connect, in a certain context, a “positive” value with behaving altruistically [16,22].

There has been considerable AI research on using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) and imitation learning to mimic human actions [23–29]. More recently, IRL has been used to learn human value systems in specific contexts such as route choice modeling [30], and multi-objective IRL/RL methods have been proposed for

human value alignment [31–34]. Here, we use IRL to address the question: Can an AI agent learn cultural values and behavioral tendencies for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups, and then generalize this learning to a new context?

1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture

IRL is a technique developed in the field of machine learning [23,26,28] to enable an AI agent to learn a new skill from human demonstrations; rather than simply mimicking the exact actions of the human, IRL attempts to learn a “reward function” from the human demonstrations. The reward function assigns positive or negative values (“rewards” or “penal- ties”) to particular features of the observed demonstration. By learning an appropriate reward function that is aligned with the presumed reward function being used by the human in the demonstrations, the AI agent is able to imitate the human behavior and generalize beyond simple mimicry of human actions.

In this article, we show how IRL can be used to train AI agents that can infer and learn the reward functions of humans belonging to a particular cultural group. The learned reward function assigns specific reward values (positive or negative) to situations in which the AI agent may find itself. By choosing actions that optimize total expected reward based on its learned reward function, the AI agent can behave in a manner that is attuned to the human culture in which it is embed- ded. A major advantage of this approach is that the agent can assign reward values to scenarios that are related to but not exactly the same as previously encountered scenarios. This allows the agent to generalize its decision-making ability, including those involving societal values and behavioral tendencies, to novel situations, emulating the first- and second- order generalization children demonstrate based on cultural learning and experience [16,35,36].

To test this approach, we collected data from human participants playing an online game involving simple forms of altruism and fairness, both of which have been regarded as important components of human morality and culture [37–42]. We recruited participants with cultural backgrounds associated with different altruistic behaviors [43], specifically, US participants who identified as White or as Latino (we use the term “Latino” to denote anyone born in or with ancestors from Latin America, regardless of gender).

Our hypothesis regarding differences in altruistic behavior is motivated by prior work showing that, within the same nation-state (here, the US), Latinos tend to be more collectivistic [44,45] and tend to have a more interdependent self- concept than Whites, who are generally characterized as being more individualistic and prone to seeing themselves as independent of others [46–48]. Whereas individualistic cultures tend to emphasize personal achievement and autonomy, collectivist cultures often prioritize group harmony and the achievements and welfare of others over personal gains. We hypothesize that, within the novel context of our online “world,” Latinos will exhibit more altruistic behavior than Whites. That is, subgroups of people within the US share cultural tendencies that they may bring to an online game, which we hypothesize can be inferred through IRL by an AI agent.

Note that rather than using data from human adults who make explicit value judgments, as in the case of the Moral Machines experiment involving the ethics of self-driving cars [49] or the trolley problem [50,51] (see also [52]), or data from descriptive judgments of scenarios presenting values and norms, as in the Delphi experiment [53] (see also [4,5]), our approach seeks to emulate the way a human child absorbs values and norms implicitly from observation and inter- actions with people in their culture [16,54]. Our approach shares similarities with previous work in reinforcement learn- ing [55,56] and brain-inspired affective learning [57] showing how an AI agent could learn ethical characteristics such as altruism, empathy, and sympathy; these approaches did not train their AI agent on human data (as in our study here) and did not focus on cultural differences. Finally, our online game scenario includes an element of fairness, in addition to altruism: the “privileged” player has to endure some costs to help the other player who has been placed in an unfair and potentially less rewarding circumstance. We hypothesize that the reward functions inferred by IRL from human data may implicitly capture this aspect of the game scenario.

To summarize, this article tests the following hypotheses:

H1: Cultural background and exposure to altruistic behavior may influence participants’ tendencies to display altruistic

behavior.

H2: An AI agent using inverse reinforcement learning can learn culturally attuned, quantifiable altruistic behaviors and

values for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups.

H3: An AI agent can generalize learned social values and behavior to a new context.

The remainder of the article focuses on the above hypothesis and presents our results. We first describe our experimen- tal studies with humans and present results showing that an AI agent can learn from observing the behavior of a particular cultural group (Latinos or Whites) an IRL reward function capturing the altruistic tendencies of that group. We then show how this learning can generalize to new problems involving other altruistic decision making. Taken together, these results, though obtained from a simple online game scenario, point the way towards future AI agents that could become attuned to the culture in which they are operating by continually learning and adjusting their behavior from observing and interacting with humans within that culture.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
From Observation to Generalization: How AI Agents Learn Culturally Specific Altruism through Inverse Reinforcement Learning

News Article:  
A recent study published in PLOS One provides compelling evidence that AI agents can absorb culture-specific values about altruism simply by observing human decisions within video game environments. By closely studying the choices made by participants from different cultural backgrounds, these agents demonstrated an ability to align with the distinctive altruistic tendencies present in those human groups. This advance directly resonates with the ongoing research focus on enabling AI systems to not just mimic, but understand and generalize social norms specific to their embedded cultural context.

The implications of this finding are profound for AI research, revealing a plausible foundation for the development of AI systems that adaptively align with the pluralism of real-world human values. At the heart of this development is the recognition that universal morality, as embodied in a “one-size-fits-all” approach by large language models and other state-of-the-art systems, encounters intrinsic limitations due to the diversity of norms across cultures. Instead, the latest research contends that AI agents should learn values, such as altruism, through immersion—by observing, interpreting, and internalizing the norms and behaviors exhibited by the actual societies in which they operate.

The research adopts the term “culture” to denote the evolving values, norms, and behaviors that characterize social groups. It posits that effective AI value alignment cannot be prescriptively encoded; rather, it must emerge through dynamic, contextualized learning akin to the processes by which children internalize social values through exposure and participation. Hence, endowing AI with the capability to adapt to the behavioral variability inherent in cultural cycles is positioned as both a necessity and a formidable technical challenge.

Central to this methodological framework is the use of **inverse reinforcement learning (IRL)**. Unlike direct imitation or hand-crafted rule systems, IRL enables an AI agent to infer the implicit reward functions driving observed human behavior. By extracting underlying preferences and value systems from human demonstration, the AI learns not only to replicate actions but also to generalize them to novel contexts within the bounds of the learned cultural logic. This flexibility is critical: the agent develops a capacity for first-order and even second-order generalization, mirroring the adaptability seen in human children who absorb values through sustained interaction within specific cultural milieus.

Crucially, the research empirically tests this approach by engaging participants from distinct US cultural backgrounds—specifically those identifying as White or Latino—in an online game structured to elicit behaviors around altruism and fairness. Drawing on established findings that Latinos in the US typically exhibit more collectivistic and interdependent self-concepts than Whites, the study hypothesizes and confirms that these culturally contingent altruistic behaviors are discernible in game choices. When exposed to these demonstrations, AI agents trained through IRL were able to internalize and quantifiably reproduce the altruistic tendencies characteristic of each cultural group.

This outcome reinforces three central hypotheses: (H1) that cultural background and exposure to altruistic behavior shape observed altruistic actions; (H2) that IRL-trained AI agents can learn specific, quantifiable social values from such behavioral data; and (H3) that AI agents can generalize these learned values and behaviors to previously unencountered scenarios. The experimental paradigm, which eschews explicit value-judgment modeling (such as moral trolley dilemmas) in favor of implicit, observational learning, thus provides a powerful analogue to natural socialization processes.

The technical significance of this research lies in its demonstration that IRL furnishes AI agents with a mechanism to infer and optimize for culturally situated reward functions—functions that undergird not only surface-level behaviors but also deeper normative commitments, such as fairness in disadvantageous situations. This is particularly salient for the ongoing challenge of value alignment, which requires AI systems to function adaptively in environments where normative expectations are not static or monolithic.

As AI agents increasingly move from controlled experimental settings to complex, diverse real-world deployments, the ability to internalize and act upon culturally specific norms through observation—rather than pre-programmed universals—may be essential for their safe, ethical, and effective integration. The path outlined by this research suggests that achieving such adaptability is feasible through rigorous application of IRL to culturally indexed human behavior.

For computer science researchers invested in the technical and ethical arc of AI development, this study presents both a foundational advance and a blueprint for further investigation. The integration of inverse reinforcement learning with cross-cultural behavioral data marks a significant inflection in the discourse on AI alignment. Readers seeking detailed methodologies, replicable code, and full experimental results are encouraged to access the complete research article and associated resources at [https://dx.doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0337914](https://dx.doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0337914). As the field confronts increasingly nuanced alignment challenges, targeted engagement with such empirical work is essential for shaping the next generation of culturally aware artificial intelligence.

title: Resolving to spend less time on your smartphone Understanding your travel habits can help, say researchers
abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habits in contemporary society—in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants’ trips for two weeks (N=419 participants; n=27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n=7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.
introduction:
Habits are a direct and deep-rooted driver of human behavior1,2. By automating our cognition, habitual processes can make the world easier to navigate. Yet, they can also conflict with our goals3,4. For better and for worse, the degree to which behavior is performed habitually has powerful implications for how we engage with the world around us. Although people engage in a wide variety of habits in daily life, habits are typically studied in isolation5. By zooming out from this standard scope, we can reveal broader, multiplicative ways that habitual processes underlie real-world behavior across domains of everyday life.

Habitual behaviors are cued and performed automatically after being frequently rewarded in stable contexts2. In turn, the strength of a habit is indicated by the level of frequency (i.e., number of occurrences) and automaticity (i.e., lack of conscious processes)6. In contemporary society, spatial and smartphone habits act as cornerstone habits. Although a variety of factors can contribute to mobility choices and mobile phone use in a given situation, past research across disciplines indicates that habit is a leading predictor of both7,8. Critically, the interaction between these two types of habits is ideally suited for analyzing interactive habitual processes in daily life, as smartphone habits are uniquely activated across spaces9,10. Exploring their interactive relationship is also vital given the growing integration of mobility and technology: a convergence with implications for numerous facets of human behavior11–16.

Spatial habits are a prominent form of habitual behavior, as people often select and engage with their everyday spaces out of habit7,8. Scholars have extensively examined the role of habit in the selection of routes17–21, or how individuals travel from an origin to a destination via a particular mode of transportation (e.g., walking). Here, we focus on the role of habit in how people select routes as well as destinations, which together comprise the activity spaces that people inhabit in daily life22. Thus, spatial habits are comprised of places (i.e., destinations) and how individuals move between them (i.e., routes). Spatial habits can be cued by myriad factors (e.g., time, trip purpose)23. Research in cognitive geography has investigated spatial habits through the study of navigation, spatial cognition, and mental mapping. A notable example is anchor-point theory, which examines how people perceive and represent space through reference points like landmarks or neighborhoods24. Moreover, the centrality of spatial behavior in past habit research reflects the repetitive nature of daily mobility patterns identified in large-scale computational studies25,26. Spatial habits thereby have unique potential to operate as foundational habits: they determine the stable contexts that offer cues (or 'triggers') for other daily habits.

Smartphone habits are another dominant form of habitual behavior in modern society, as well as a heuristic case in habit research27. By bundling many possible behaviors (e.g., social media scrolling, podcast listening) into one handheld object, smartphones facilitate the development of a wide variety of activation cues (e.g., times, user activities), including personalized sets of cues tied to specific apps28. Accordingly, smartphone habits can be uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts9,10—challenging the presumed 'context-dependence' of traditional media habits and presumed role of location for cueing habits at-large2. However, some studies reveal that certain types of apps are also more likely to be used in certain types of places, as routes and destinations afford certain activities29,30. In this way, specific smartphone behaviors may develop implicit associations with specific types of spaces31,32—such as more habitually selected contexts33. In other words, spatial habits have the potential to make smartphone habits stronger.

To test this proposition, we examine whether more habitual apps are used disproportionately more in (1) habitually travelled routes and (2) habitually visited destinations. We test whether habitual apps can be interwoven with transportation habits by evaluating app use on routes. For example, app use and specific spaces may become embedded in scripts34: a habitual route (e.g., driving from home to work) can cue an app habit (e.g., listening to Spotify) at rehearsed points during the trip (e.g., traffic lights). These scripts may also be prompted by specific cues (e.g., time of day, social setting), thereby coupling spatial habits (e.g., the morning commute) and smartphone habits (e.g., listening to a news podcast) through sequences of cued actions. Additionally, we test the downstream influence of spatial habits on smartphone use by evaluating app use in destinations (e.g., the workplace). Since spaces chosen out of habit are more familiar35, habitually visited destinations may be more likely to contain familiar cues that activate app habits (e.g., checking a social media app while cleaning the kitchen). Altogether, we test whether app habits are activated more when individuals choose routes and destinations out of habit.

We further examine the robustness of the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits by exploring the distinctive role of social vs. non-social apps. Social apps provide a direct channel for social needs and norms to be activated habitually in daily life36. Based on the potency of social rewards in habit formation, along with the high proportion of social app usage in studies of overall smartphone usage37,38, social apps are expected to exhibit higher habit strength27,39. The theorized strength of social app habits may derive from their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues40. For example, people may habitually check social apps throughout the day in response to or in anticipation of new messages27,37. Thus, social app habits may be more likely to be context-independent: triggered across a variety of spatial contexts39.

Habits present a unique measurement challenge because they are triggered and performed with minimal conscious awareness throughout daily life41. Traditionally, habit research has centered on establishing the causal role of habit versus more conscious processes such as goals27. Here, moving beyond the question of whether habits or goals matter more, we focus on the real-world conditions—or contexts—under which habits become more likely to be activated. Accordingly, the current study sought to capture habits in the real world. By coupling GPS and app logs with map-based questionnaires, we collected objective and subjective indicators of spatial and smartphone habits and used those indicators to predict app use in context.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Study Reveals Smartphone App Use Intensifies in Habitually Traveled Spaces, Unveiling Interdependent Patterns of Spatial and Digital Habits

News Article:  
Smartphone habits are stronger in spaces chosen out of habit, according to a new large-scale study that uncovers how daily mobility patterns and app use co-evolve to shape human behavior (see "Smartphone habits are stronger in spaces chosen out of habit - PMC"). By demonstrating that smartphone app use intensifies along habitually traveled routes and at habitually visited destinations, this research reframes the mechanics of digital interaction in contemporary society, providing a rigorous empirical basis for understanding the multiplicative effects of spatial and smartphone habits [Abstract, Citation].

Prior investigations into habitual processes frequently isolated specific behaviors or domains. This study challenges that paradigm by employing a dual lens on spatial and smartphone habits—cornerstone components of everyday life whose interplay has historically been under-analyzed. Leveraging a custom mobile application, the research team logged mobility and smartphone app usage data, in conjunction with map-based trip questionnaires, from 419 participants over two weeks, amassing more than 27 million GPS and app records and over 7,000 trip surveys. Their central discovery: the strength of a participant’s habitual app use predicted both a higher likelihood and greater intensity of app engagement when individuals were on more habitually traveled routes or at more habitually chosen destinations [Abstract].

The study’s high-resolution behavioral logging adds credence and granularity to prior theoretical assertions from cognitive geography and habit formation literature. Spatial habits—involving repeated selection of routes and destinations—emerge as context-providing substrates: they serve as stable behavioral scaffolds that trigger other domain-specific habits, such as digital interactions, within these familiar physical environments [Introduction]. In this framework, spatial habits not only exhibit a tendency toward automatic, cue-driven repetition, but also amplify the enactment of intersecting smartphone habits.

Crucially, the research operationalizes habit strength using both objective and subjective indicators, mitigating reliance on self-report by synchronizing mobile sensing (GPS and app logs) with experience sampling via trip maps. Findings demonstrate that the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective habit indicators—namely, frequently traveled routes and regularly visited places, as logged by the app [Abstract]. These results extend prior computational studies on mobility by showing that not only are mobility patterns highly repetitive, but their confluence with app habits produces quantifiably increased digital engagement in those same spatial contexts.

A further layer of nuance emerges when differentiating between social and non-social app habits. The study finds that social app habits are both stronger in magnitude and display greater context-independence compared to non-social app habits. Social app engagement is less tightly coupled to specific spatial contexts, plausibly due to social apps’ reliance on internal cues such as anticipated communication—contrasting with non-social app habits that are more likely to be modulated by physical location or routine [Introduction, Abstract]. This domain-sensitive distinction reveals not only the robustness but also the specificity of interactive habitual processes across categories of digital behavior.

Expert-level implications extend well beyond habit measurement. For researchers and practitioners in ubiquitous computing and context-aware systems, these findings underscore the necessity of integrating spatial habit analysis into models predicting app engagement. The multiplicative relationship between spatial and smartphone habits challenges static or singular-context models, indicating that real-world digital interventions and recommendations must take into account both users’ recurring mobility patterns and their app-specific habitual trajectories. For urban technologists, the results suggest designing adaptive digital services and public interfaces that recognize individuals’ spatial routines as powerful, dynamically evolving cues for digital behavior [Bridge Constraint].

This work also brings methodological advancements by demonstrating the value of linking mobile sensing with in situ survey instruments to ground computational models of habit in real-world contexts. Such methodological synthesis is indispensable for advancing habit research from laboratory abstraction to ecologically valid behavioral prediction.

By clarifying how habitually selected contexts—routes and destinations—provide scaffolding for digital engagement, this study opens new inquiries into how urban environments and technology platforms can be co-designed to either leverage or intervene in habitual processes. Given the entrenchment of both spatial and smartphone habits in daily life, future research may explore interventions that reshape habitual routines, enhance digital wellbeing, or design new cues for context-appropriate digital engagement.

For an in-depth analysis of these findings and methodological details, readers are encouraged to examine the full research article at [https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2](https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2).

title: An AI approach for singleimagebased 3D character animation with preserved proportions
abstract: Reconstructing object deformation from a single image remains a significant challenge in computer vision and graphics. Existing methods typically rely on multi-view video to recover deformation, limiting their applicability under constrained scenarios. To address this, we propose DeformSplat, a novel framework that effectively guides 3D Gaussian deformation from only a single image. Our method introduces two main technical contributions. First, we present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching which bridges the domain gap between 3D Gaussian representations and 2D pixel observations. This enables robust deformation guidance from sparse visual cues. Second, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation consisting of initialization and refinement. This segmentation explicitly identifies rigid regions, crucial for maintaining geometric coherence during deformation. By combining these two techniques, our approach can reconstruct consistent deformations from a single image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods and naturally extends to various applications, such as frame interpolation and interactive object manipulation. Project page : https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat
introduction:
Introduction

Reconstructing object deformation from visual data is essential for creating realistic and immersive content in various media fields, such as virtual reality (VR), film, and gaming [Shuai et al. 2022]. As part of this effort, recent works have explored photorealistic rendering of deformable objects, aiming to better capture their ap- pearance and motion over time [Lu et al. 2024a; Wu et al. 2024b]. Although these methods have demonstrated strong capabilities for dynamic scene reconstruction, they often rely on multi-view or tem- porally continuous video data. Such data can be difficult to capture in real-world settings, which limits their practical applicability. This motivates the development of methods that reconstruct deformation from minimal visual inputs.

Toachievehigh-qualityandefficientreconstructionofdeformable objects, recent research has focused on scene representations that support both photorealism and editability. Among these, 3D Gauss- ian Splatting (3DGS) [Kerbl et al. 2023] has gained attention for its high-quality rendering and fast inference. Unlike implicit neural representations, such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) [Mildenhall

SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, December 15–18, 2025, Hong Kong, Hong Kong.

Trovato et al.

et al. 2021], 3DGS explicitly models geometry, making it both in- terpretable and easy to manipulate. Motivated by these advantages, several subsequent works have explored Gaussian-based scene edit- ing. For example, several approaches leveraging diffusion models enable intuitive editing guided by text prompts [Wu et al. 2024a] or reference images [Mei et al. 2024]. While these approaches enable intuitive edits, diffusion models often produce inconsistent results, as ambiguous text prompts lead to varied interpretations and limited geometric control. Another recent method, GESI [Luo et al. 2024], aims to address detailed geometric editing directly using a single reference image. However, it encounters difficulties in preserving intricate geometry under long-range deformations, often altering the original structure significantly. These limitations motivate our key research question: Can 3D Gaussians be deformed from a single image while preserving the original geometry?

In this work, we aim to deform a pre-reconstructed 3D Gaussian representation using only a single target image depicting a defor- mation, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Our setting is challenging, as we aim to deform 3D Gaussians using only a single RGB image, unlike conventional methods that rely on richer inputs such as multiple views or video sequences. The absence of depth and camera pose information further complicates the deformation process in our case. This constrained setting gives rise to two key challenges. The first challenge is determining how and in which direction the Gaussians should deform when only a single viewpoint is available. This is difficult because a single image provides only partial observations of the 3D structure, making it hard to infer meaningful deformation cues. To extract meaningful deformation cues under this constraint, it is essential to establish reliable correspondences between the 2D image and the 3D Gaussians. The second challenge is to prevent overfitting to the single input image, which can result in unwanted geometric distortions due to the lack of depth or multi-view con- straints. Thus, preserving original geometry is crucial, especially in rigid regions that should remain unchanged during deformation.

To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework called DeformSplat, consisting of two main components: Gaussian-to- Pixel Matching and Rigid Part Segmentation. Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching aims to guide the deformation by linking visually similar regions between the 3D Gaussians and the target image. Specifically, we render multi-view images from the 3D Gaussians and compute pixel-wise correspondences between each rendered image and the target image using an image matcher. Based on the pixel-to-pixel matching, we select the viewpoint with the largest visual overlap, and translate its correspondences into Gaussian-to-Pixel mappings. This step provides an essential basis for deformation, enabling the Gaussians to reflect the geometry depicted in the target image.

To further ensure geometric consistency, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation that explicitly identifies rigid regions within the Gaussian representation. To achieve this, our method first initial- izes rigid groups based on Gaussian-to-Pixel correspondences and then iteratively refines these groups during optimization. The seg- mentation is used in a rigidity-aware optimization that regularizes rigid and non-rigid regions differently to preserve geometry. Conse- quently, we achieve superior performance than previous SOTA and generalize to applications such as frame interpolation and interac- tive object manipulation.

SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, December 15–18, 2025, Hong Kong, Hong Kong.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

We propose DeformSplat, a novel framework for rigidity- aware deformation of 3D Gaussians using only a single target image.

We present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching strategy that con- nects the 3D Gaussian representation with the 2D target image to guide deformation.

WeproposeRigidPartSegmentationmethod,whichpreserves the original geometry by detecting rigid regions and con- straining their deformation.

Our method shows superior performance in single image Gaussian deformation and extends to applications such as frame interpolation and interactive manipulation.

Headline:  
DeformSplat: Rigidity-Aware 3D Gaussian Deformation from a Single Image—Responsible Innovation Amid Evolving Intellectual Property Constraints

News Article:  
In the current landscape of digital innovation and disseminated content, intellectual property (IP) governance is pivotal. As reinforced by Elsevier B.V., which asserts ownership of all site content—including materials subject to text and data mining and AI training—the creation and usage of new digital methodologies must operate within established legal and ethical frameworks. Real-world applications such as mesh deformation-based, single-view 3D reconstruction for modeling consumer products, including full-frame eyeglasses from a single RGB image, underscore the demand for technical advances that responsibly leverage and transform digital content [Elsevier B.V. 2026]. This context accentuates the relevance of DeformSplat—a rigidity-aware single-image 3D Gaussian deformation framework—as both a boundary-pushing technical milestone and an exemplar of responsible, IP-conscious digital content creation.

To date, the computer vision and graphics communities have confronted significant obstacles in reconstructing object deformations from sparse visual data. While photorealistic rendering of deformable objects has advanced with methods dependent on multi-view or temporally continuous video data, these approaches are hampered by practical restrictions on data acquisition [Abstract; Introduction]. Single-image-based solutions present a highly constrained scenario, where traditional pipelines reliant on multi-view geometry and depth information are rendered inapplicable.

DeformSplat emerges as a direct response to these constraints, targeting the question of whether explicit 3D Gaussian splattings—a scene representation known for high-quality rendering and explicit geometric modeling [Kerbl et al. 2023]—can be robustly deformed using only a single, novel-view image while maintaining fidelity to original geometric structures [Abstract; Introduction].

The cornerstone of DeformSplat is its dual-innovation architecture:

**1. Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching:**  
This module operationalizes the linkage between a reconstructed 3D Gaussian representation and a 2D target image. DeformSplat renders multi-view images from the 3D Gaussians, establishing dense pixel-wise correspondences between these views and the input image. Visual overlap is then maximized by selecting the rendered view with the greatest correspondence, translating these pixel-level alignments into a direct Gaussian-to-pixel mapping. This foundation is crucial for inferring deformation cues from the limited 2D observation, addressing the first core challenge—the determination of deformation directionality and extent in the single-view regime [Abstract; Introduction].

**2. Rigid Part Segmentation:**  
The second key innovation explicitly focuses on preserving geometric consistency, especially within regions that should remain physically invariant under deformation. Initial rigid groups are established based on the Gaussian-to-pixel correspondences. These groups are subsequently refined iteratively during the optimization process, segmenting the reconstructed object into rigid and non-rigid components. Rigidity-aware optimization constrains deformations for these segmented regions, thereby regularizing the transformation of rigid parts to prevent overfitting and to ensure geometric plausibility even in the absence of depth or multi-view constraints [Abstract; Introduction].

Extensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that DeformSplat meaningfully surpasses prevailing methods in the domain of single-image Gaussian deformation, effectively safeguarding original geometry even under long-range or complex deformations [Abstract]. The technique generalizes across applications that extend far beyond the research laboratory, including frame interpolation and interactive object manipulation—use cases integral to interactive media, VR content generation, and digital asset editing [Abstract].

The significance of DeformSplat’s framework is thus twofold:  
First, it expands the frontier of what is computationally attainable under constrained input regimes, directly addressing the practical challenges posed by real-world scenarios where multi-view video or rich depth data are unavailable. Second, and equally crucial in the current copyright-sensitive era, the approach embraces methodologies that are intrinsically respectful of IP protocols. By conditioning the deformation process on robust, explainable correspondences and explicit rigidity segmentation—rather than ambiguous or generative-only approaches—DeformSplat provides a level of transparency and traceability beneficial for legal compliance and content provenance. This directly parallels current licensing and usage policies, such as those articulated by Elsevier B.V., which govern how source content may be accessed, transformed, or redistributed [Elsevier B.V. 2026].

In integrating responsible content manipulation with mathematically rigorous, interpretable algorithms, DeformSplat exemplifies a mode of digital content creation that is aligned with both the technical and regulatory demands of next-generation computer vision research.

For subject-matter experts seeking to advance applications in 3D reconstruction while maintaining compliance with evolving content regulations, the technical release of DeformSplat marks a critical resource. The research is accessible at https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat. Engage directly with the project to assess reproducibility claims, analyze architectural decisions, and integrate the framework within your own research—without compromising ethical and legal standards.

title: Digital technique puts rendered fabric in the best light
abstract: Realistic fabric rendering is still a significant challenge due to their complex structures and varying fiber properties. We present a new fabric shading technique, which models both reflection and transmission using a hybrid of ray and wave optics methods, grounded in simulation data. We target fabrics woven from yarns, each formed by twisting together one or more plies, which further contain twisted fibers. Our model is based on simulations that predict the scattering of a narrow Gaussian beam by a single ply. Comparing results from full-wave simulations and path tracing, we found that ray optics can accurately simulate the average far field scattering from an ensemble of plies, but not the variation among individual ply instances, and ray tracing overlooks important diffraction effects. Following these observations, our model is built from ray simulations performed for many ply instances, with simulation data fitted by Gaussian mixtures to be used during rendering. Wave simulations are used to calibrate noise functions that account for instance-to-instance variation, and an aperture diffraction model is used to handle light passing between plies and yarns. The result is a hybrid model capable of producing realistic appearance and highlight structure in fabrics, while capturing spatial break-ups and irregularities and simulating the subtle color shifts and blurriness that occur in transmission. We validate our results by comparing rendered images with photographs, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in achieving realistic cloth rendering.
introduction: In recent years, significant progress has been made in fabric render-ing, particularly through ply-based models [Zhu et al. 2024, 2023a]. These models have substantially improved realism by relying onphysical principles and measurable properties, rather than on em-pirical methods [Estevez and Kulla 2017; Zeltner et al . 2022] thatmatch appearance purely through observation.Despite these advancements, faithfully capturing the appearanceof fabrics remains challenging. Modeling both reflection and trans-mission accurately is difficult, and characteristic irregularities ofreal materials are often inadequately addressed. Additionally, manycurrent models rely on idealized assumptions—for instance, fibersare assumed to be perfectly circular and lack natural variation, andwave optics effects are ignored—which do not reflect the complexityof real-world fabrics.We propose a ply model derived from first principles, combiningthe realism of wave optics with the speed of ray optics.  Our ply scattering model is based on multi-fiber full-wave simulations andpath tracing, where both simulation methods account for the true,generalized shapes of fibers. Comparisons between wave and raysimulations show that ray optics can accurately compute the aver-age far field scattering from a large collection of plies, but not thevariation among individual ply instances. Thus, we model averageply scattering by fitting ray simulation results to a small set of Gauss-ian lobes, achieving a lightweight representation. Moreover, sincethe spatially varying and sometimes glinty appearance of fabricsarises from instance-to-instance differences in ply structures, wederive noise data from a limited set of wave simulations and usethem to augment our average ply scattering model.Moreover, our model handles multi-bounce interactions betweenmultiple plies and in double-yarn configurations, resulting in a new,more diffuse transmission model that captures complex scatteringand absorption effects across ply layers. Additionally, we introducewave optics based diffraction lobes to more realistically model trans-mission through gaps and around yarn edges. Together, these com-ponents enable a robust and accurate approach to fabric rendering,contributing to the advancement of appearance modeling.

Headline:  
Hybrid Ray-Wave Optics Model Sets New Standard in Photorealistic Cloth Rendering for Digital Textile Industries

News Article:  
A new hybrid ray-wave optics model for fabric shading is poised to redefine the technical limits of photorealistic cloth rendering, addressing a longstanding gap in industrial-grade digital textile and fashion visualization. Released in the context of increasing demand for realistic digital representations of woven fabrics, this technique uniquely enables the accurate simulation of both average and instance-specific light scattering—directly responding to the requirements for faithful digital fabric appearance in textile design, virtual fashion prototyping, and high-fidelity visualization workflows [(Anchor Event)](https://www.instagram.com/popular/realistic-cloth-rendering-with-a-ray-wave-hybrid-shading-model/).

The imperative for realism in digital textiles is well established. As industries push for virtual sampling and digital twins of apparel and interior materials, the inability to precisely replicate the nuanced optical properties of complex woven fabrics has remained a limiting factor. Prevailing shading models have, until now, been unable to deliver the level of physical fidelity required to meet industrial use cases—frequently over-idealizing geometry, neglecting the structural irregularities of yarns and plies, and systematically omitting wave optics effects that play a decisive role in fabric appearance.  

The newly introduced ray-wave hybrid shading model, as detailed in recent proceedings (see [Citation](https://dl.acm.org/doi/epdf/10.1145/3763359)), makes a decisive advance by integrating both ray and wave optics grounded in first-principles simulation data. This approach targets fabrics composed of yarns made from twisted plies, themselves containing intertwined fibers—recapitulating the true physical complexity encountered in real materials. Crucially, the model simulates both the reflection and transmission of light at multiple scales, employing full-wave simulations for single plies alongside path tracing for efficient estimation of average far field scattering.

Source comparisons between ray and wave simulations demonstrate that while ray optics methods can faithfully compute the average far field scattering from an ensemble of plies, they fail to capture the subtle, spatially varying scattering differences arising between individual ply instances. These micro-scale variations—essential to the recognizable glinty, irregular, and often spatially discontinuous appearance of woven textiles—are rendered invisible by conventional ray-based approaches.

To resolve this, the hybrid model employs a set of methodologically rigorous strategies:  
- **Ray-based models** are used to compute the average scattering properties by fitting simulation results to lightweight Gaussian mixture lobes, enabling efficient representation of the aggregate material behavior.
- **Wave optics simulations** provide calibration data to parameterize noise functions that introduce spatially varying, instance-specific scattering irregularities, thus instantiating the appearance diversity seen in real fabrics.
- **Aperture diffraction models** are integrated to accurately simulate transmission through inter-ply gaps and around yarn boundaries, directly representing critical effects such as subtle color shifts and transmission blurriness—phenomena previously neglected or only heuristically approximated.

This multi-component approach not only models the average look of fabric, but also systematically addresses the instance-to-instance variation that is essential for visual realism in industrial digital textile workflows. By capturing complex optical phenomena such as highlight breakup and color shift resulting from both absorption and multi-bounce scattering across multiple fabric layers and between yarns, the model delivers a marked improvement over empirically fitted or geometrically oversimplified predecessors.

Validation leverages rendered outputs against photographic references, demonstrating that the hybrid model's predictions align with real-world fabric appearance under a range of illumination and transmission scenarios. This evidence not only corroborates the physical credibility of the underlying scattering and diffraction models, but also affirms their suitability for deployment in the rigorous quality assurance and visualization demands of textile and fashion industries.

The resulting shading technique directly addresses industrial requirements for physical accuracy, visual realism, and computational tractability—key criteria in digital textile and fashion visualization pipelines. With current market trends prompting a shift towards all-digital sampling and product representation, and as the boundary between physical and digital product lines continues to erode, the need for such a technically robust, first-principles-based model has become acute.

In sum, the hybrid ray-wave optics model marks a substantive technical inflection point for fabric rendering in digital textiles. By enabling high-fidelity reproduction of both average and spatially varying light scattering in complex woven structures, it responds precisely to the core requirements of industrial visual realism, accuracy, and scalability.

For the computer graphics and digital textile research community, the full methodology, results, and rigorous validation are detailed in the referenced research paper. Researchers and practitioners aiming to advance digital textile realism are strongly encouraged to read the complete article for in-depth technical exposition and comprehensive evaluation ([Access full paper here](https://dl.acm.org/doi/epdf/10.1145/3763359)).
