A Concise History of AI: Two-Line Summaries (Part Two) [https://lnkd.in/gDutyg4Y](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgDutyg4Y&urlhash=e4Kw&trk=public_post-text) Unlocking the Evolution of Machine Learning: From Neural Nets to LLMs Dive into the captivating journey of machine learning as we explore the pivotal breakthroughs that defined the field. Building from the early models to today’s deep learning marvels, this summary provides insights that every AI enthusiast must know. Here are the highlights: Neural Networks Basics: Understand how neural nets transform input numbers through layers of neurons to deliver predictions, such as house prices or image recognition. Critical Datasets: Learn about transformative datasets like MNIST and ImageNet that fueled advancements in machine learning. Key Innovations: Discover landmark papers that solved crucial problems in model training: ResNet: Tackled vanishing gradients, enabling deeper networks. Dropout: Enhanced generalization by introducing randomness in training. The field of AI is evolving rapidly, and staying updated is essential. Engage with this digest to fuel your curiosity and spark discussions in your network! Curious to learn more? Share your thoughts and explore further! Source link [https://lnkd.in/gDutyg4Y](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FgDutyg4Y&urlhash=e4Kw&trk=public_post-text)

2mo  
[#story](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Ffeed%2Fhashtag%2Fstory&trk=public_post-text) Training convolutional neural networks with the Forward–Forward Algorithm Riccardo Scodellaro, Ajinkya Kulkarni, Frauke Alves & Matthias Schröter Max Planck Institute, University of Göttingen 2025 [https://lnkd.in/ghDPhpHv](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https%3A%2F%2Flnkd%2Ein%2FghDPhpHv&urlhash=6Fm6&trk=public_post-text) For decades, most successful image-recognition systems have relied on a single training trick: backpropagation, a mathematically powerful but biologically implausible way of teaching neural networks by sending error signals backward through the model. In 2022, Geoffrey Hinton proposed a radical alternative—the Forward–Forward (FF) algorithm—inspired more by how brains might actually learn. Instead of correcting mistakes after the fact, FF learning compares *positive* and *negative* examples directly and adjusts each layer using a simple, local measure of how “good” its activity looks. This paper asks an important question: *Can this brain-inspired idea work for modern convolutional neural networks (CNNs), the backbone of today’s image AI?* The challenge is that CNNs process images spatially—pixel by pixel—while labels (like “cat” or “airplane”) are global. To bridge this gap, the authors invent clever ways of embedding label information across the entire image. One approach uses structured Fourier patterns; another uses morphological transformations, spreading label signals spatially so every convolutional filter can “see” what class it’s learning. With these strategies, the researchers show that CNNs can indeed be trained using the Forward–Forward algorithm. On CIFAR-10, deeper networks train stably, and morphology-based labels help avoid “shortcut learning,” where models latch onto superficial cues instead of real structure. On the more challenging CIFAR-100 dataset, carefully designed label patterns scale to 100 classes without collapsing performance. By visualizing what the networks focus on using Class Activation Maps, the authors find that FF-trained CNNs learn meaningful and complementary features across layers—evidence that learning is not only stable, but also structured and interpretable. The broader implication is striking: backpropagation may not be the only viable way to train powerful vision models. Forward–Forward learning can extend beyond simple fully connected networks into realistic CNNs, opening doors to more biologically plausible AI, better alignment with neuromorphic hardware, and new ways of thinking about how artificial and natural intelligence might converge.
